overview.title: How to read Genesis.
overview.content:
If you’re like most people, you’ve wondered about the deep questions of life: Why are we here? What is life all about? Genesis takes you back to the beginning of time to find the answers. It tells about many beginnings: the first plants and animals; the first man and woman; the first sin; the first news of God’s salvation. It also shows God’s dealings with Noah, Abraham, Joseph, and others, repeatedly demonstrating his desire to have fellowship with his people.

Genesis is an exciting book, full of unforgettable stories that introduce fascinating people and detail remarkable events. It is an important book to read, for it is the foundation of all that follows in the pages of Scripture. In the first 11 chapters of Genesis you’ll get a big-picture view, covering four major events: the creation, the fall of humanity, the flood, and the record of nations that came into existence afterward. In the next 39 chapters (12-50) you’ll get a close-up view of one family—Abraham, Isaac, Jacob/Israel and his descendants. God called this family to himself so that through them he might eventually bless all the families of the earth (12:2).

As you read Genesis, take special notice of the focus Genesis places on the relationship between God and humanity—broken in the garden and restored through sacrifices and personal encounters with God. Through the stories of history Genesis illustrates cycles of sin and repentance—and continually shows God at work to accomplish his plans.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it around 1446–1406 bc. He relied on revelation from God and, perhaps, earlier oral or written records.

overview.title: To whom was it written?
overview.content: Because it announces that “all the families on earth will be blessed” (12:3), it seems fair to conclude that Moses believed all people could benefit from its message.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To show that when God made the creation, it was good. Genesis goes on to say that when sin entered the world, it corrupted the creation and led God to initiate his plan for salvation.

overview.title: What period of history does it cover?
overview.content: From the time of creation (a date that cannot be known for sure) to the time when the Israelites arrived in Egypt and grew into a nation (about 1800 bc).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Reading Genesis in SourceView format will help you discover some new insights into this book. Consider, for example, the 100 times God speaks: 38 of these occurrences appear in the first 11 chapters which focus on the events from creation through Babel; 44 are found in chapters 12 to 23 which focus on the life of Abraham; and the remainder—18 occurrences—are located in those chapters which deal with Isaac, Jacob/Israel, and Joseph (chapter 24 to the end). The emphasis is clearly on God’s words to Abraham. Though more than half of the book is dedicated to the affairs of Abraham’s descendants, less than one-fifth of God’s spoken communication occurs in this part of the book.

God speaks the most to Abraham (1,138 words; see God 39-48,53-62,66-72,75,77-82); second most to Noah (648 words; see God 29-36); and third most to Jacob/Israel (320 words; see God 87-88,90-100). He also speaks to at least a dozen other individuals including four women, a murderer, a slave, and a pagan king. Can you discover who they all are?

In addition to God there are another 61 individuals or groups who form the speaking cast of this very dramatic book! The four leading roles (represented in green) are Abraham, Isaac, Israel (Jacob), and Joseph. A focus on the longest speaking part of each of these reveals some very interesting insights. Each of the first three reserve their longest speech for the end of their life to address issues about their children and future generations (see Abraham 38, Isaac 27, and Jacob/Israel 82). In contrast, Joseph’s longest speech comes early in his life and has to do with the interpretation of Pharaoh’s dream (Joseph 19).

Among the supporting cast (in blue), some of the larger speeches that stand out are:
• the faith-filled testimony of a nameless slave (see Abraham’s Servant 11—461 words),
• the self-sacrificing commitment of a repentant man (see Judah 13—395 words),
• the desperate cry of a pagan king for revelation (see Pharaoh (Joseph’s) 3—155 words), and
• the anguished report of men trapped by their past (see Joseph’s Brothers 14—126 words).

overview.title: How to read Exodus.
overview.content:
A spectacular escape and a hair-raising chase scene are only two of the many epic adventures in Exodus. But this book is much more than an adventure series; it recounts the supernatural rescue of an entire people by their God. Despite God’s intervention, the Israelites seem unable to remain loyal to him. Seeing their faltering faith can actually encourage us with a vivid lesson: Even imperfect people can get to know God, who loves them perfectly.

Exodus can be separated into two sections. The first 19 chapters describe the Israelites’ journey from bondage to deliverance, and the last 21 chapters record laws by which God wanted his people to live as they worshiped him and followed him. As you read Exodus, be alert to the marvelous teachings about God contained within its pages. God is revealed as powerful, caring, awesome in glory and majesty and worthy of exuberant worship, forgiving and merciful, and passionately loving.

Exodus spotlights God’s direct involvement in human history—how he goes to astonishing lengths to reach those he loves. For example, he helps Moses overcome an incredible inferiority complex to become a great leader in Israel. Repeatedly, Exodus shows how much God enjoys people who, aware of their weaknesses, trust him as their only hope.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it around 1440 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To remind the Israelites how God had rescued them from oppression.

overview.title: What was its historical setting?
overview.content: Jacob had migrated to Egypt with his family to escape a famine in Canaan some four centuries earlier. But the “land of escape” became a “land of bondage” as the Egyp-tians sought to maintain dominance over the Israelites by forcing them into slave labor. Shortly before his death in the desert, Moses reflected on and penned the swirling events of Israel’s escape from Egypt.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
How to read Leviticus.
overview.content:
Rules . . . who needs them? A fast look around and within gives us a quick, clear answer: WE DO! The Israelites have known nothing but slavery for hundreds of years; God knows they must learn to govern themselves and build a nation. He pulls his people aside into the wilderness to give them the clarity they need to live with justice, good health, loving families, honest business, wholesome arts, etc. So while Leviticus may seem like an out-of-date legal document full of distasteful customs, it’s really about maintaining a right relationship with God and neighbor.

As you read Leviticus, think about how important holy living is to God. Be alert to the essential truths the book teaches about God, and let it challenge you to walk more closely and more humbly with your God, as you hear his call: “Be holy because I . . . am holy” (19:2).

You’ll discover that God wants us to be free from sin and its fatal effects. You’ll find that he wants to have a personal relationship with us. You’ll see that he wants us to be set apart for him.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it around 1440 bc.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: God wanted the people of Israel to have instructions for their social and religious life. He wanted to show them how to live in harmony with each other and with him.

overview.title: What was its historical setting?
overview.content: Moses had led Israel out of Egypt into the desert. At the foot of Mount Sinai, they built the tabernacle to worship God. It was a vital time to train them to listen, to obey, and to learn to function as a people without the influences of other nations and their ways. While they waited for orders to march toward the promised land, God gave these instructions so they could build their lives and their nation on strong foundations.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
As you turn the pages of this book, one thing the SourceView reader will notice is how red this book is! In fact God’s words occupy more than 88 percent of the text of Leviticus. The Narrator’s comments make up almost 9 percent of the book. That leaves only a little over 3 percent for the 11 green sections which record Moses’ words, and the one blue section that reflects the words of Aaron.

Moses’ opening speech sets the stage for the rest of the book: “This is what the Lord has commanded us to do!” (Moses 1). Five more times he underscores that what he is saying has been commanded by God (once each in Moses 4,5,9, and twice in Moses 2). He makes it clear these regulations are not of human origin. They are from God and they are very purposeful. Moses explains the intended result of these commandments. He says that they are given:
• “to purify you” (Moses 2);
• to make you right with God (Moses 2,5); and
• “so that the glory of the Lord may appear to you” (Moses 4).

The Hebrew verb tsavah means “to command.” It is used of God 24 times throughout Leviticus. It is used in 7:36,38a,38b; 8:4,5,9,13,17,21,29,34,35,36; 9:6,7,10; 10:1,13,15; 16:34; 17:2; 24:23; 25:21; 27:34. It’s no surprise that the final words of the Narrator echo Moses’ opening statement: “These are the commands that the Lord gave through Moses on Mount Sinai for the Israelites” (Narrator 49). This aptly summarizes the book.

Yet in the heart of this account of God’s concern with justice and holiness, there is a wonderful discovery to be made. Observe the only blue text in the book (Aaron 1). It is a plea from Aaron to show mercy to his sons who are grieving the death of their brothers. The God who so carefully had laid out the rules for the priesthood readily understands and extends to them compassion. This visual statement—the sole blue text in a sea of red—adds to the picture laid out in the SourceView format of a God who responds in relationship and mercy while bringing definition and clarity.

overview.title: How to read Numbers.
overview.content:
A title like Numbers sounds about as exciting as “Dictionary” or “Phone Book.” That’s not the case at all! Get ready for a surprise. This book is loaded with powerful stories of Israel’s rebellion and God’s judgment and mercy.

Numbers begins at Mount Sinai, as the Israelites wait on God for directions to the promised land. As the book unfolds, be alert to the change in attitude from eager anticipation to petty grumbling and outright revolt. Numbers ends with a new generation of Israelites about to enter the promised land. As you study Numbers, reflect on the temptations in your own life to grumble, complain, or rebel. Consider as well the lengths to which God goes to restore his people.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it about 40 years after the exodus from Egypt (1406 bc).

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: To God’s people—the children of Israel. Numbers is Moses’ memoirs about the Israelites’ 40 years in the desert. He wrote it to document their story, a story of their past to shape their future, to show God’s judgment against sin, in the context of his constant faithfulness and patience with his beloved Israel.

overview.title: Where does it fit in the big story?
overview.content: Numbers covers almost 40 years—specifically, 38 years and 9 months—of Israel’s wanderings in the desert, from Mount Sinai to the Jordan River. After Israel came out of Egypt, Moses sent spies into Canaan. The spies’ report was met with fear rather than faith. So for every day the spies spent scouting in Canaan, the people spent one year wandering in judgment for their unfaithfulness (14:33-34).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The picture of Numbers on the SourceView pages brings the story alive! The multi-colored pages show an extensive cast of charcters interacting in this unfolding adventure. These 13 individuals include: Moses, his brother and brother-in-law, two pagan kings, a priest, a prophet, a couple of rebels, a young man, two spies, and—perhaps most surprising—a talking donkey! Their names (in alphabetical order) are: Aaron, Abiram, Balaam, Balaam’s donkey, Caleb, Dathan, Eleazar, Hobab, Joshua, King Balak, the King of Edom, Moses, and a young man.

Eleven groups also speak in Numbers. They include: two groups of spies (ten in one, twelve in the other), the ancient poets, the Gadites, the Israelites, the Korahites, the Manassehites, the military commanders, the Moabite officials, the Reubenites, and Zelophehad’s daughters.

But amidst all these voices, God’s words stand out. He speaks 80 times. The longest section spoken by God is his 73rd statement. Leviticus was concerned with forming the people’s ways through the structure of laws and principles; Numbers fills in the heart and soul of the nation, with instructions on how they should celebrate their life together as a people. These festivals would cement their identity as God’s people and give them multi-generational continuity as a nation.

overview.title: How to read Deuteronomy.
overview.content:
This book deals with hardship, testing, and doubt, but also with promise, hope, and trust. It reminds us that faith is not automatic or mechanical. It shows us that faith becomes personal and active when it springs from a living relationship with a loving God. The message of Deuteronomy can be summed up in five words: “Devote yourself wholeheartedly to God.” No wonder it’s so relevant to the church today!

As Deuteronomy begins, the Israelites are about to cross the Jordan River into Canaan. One of the most momentous eras of history is drawing to a close. Moses reminds all the people of the many awesome things their great God has done for them in bringing them out of Egypt. Moses’ speech is often full of profound praise. He speaks of what God has done to redeem his people. Through these actions, God reveals his caring heart for the poor, the widows, the orphans, and the aliens.

The covenant that God made with the Israelites in this book is similar in many respects to the treaties ancient Middle Eastern kings made with their subjects. Such treaties were made when a king imposed certain obligations on his vassals. Vassals were foreign servants, sometimes kings themselves, who were compelled to obey the treaty. Deuteronomy’s format suggests that the Israelites were servants to the King of Kings—and it may be helpful as you read to think of this book as an example of an agreement between a king and his people.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Moses wrote it around 1406 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: Deuteronomy records Moses’ final words to the Israelites before they entered the promised land. He challenged them to obey the Lord faithfully and reject all forms of idolatry—a call to the new generation to renew formally the earlier covenant with God that their parents had broken.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: The Egyp-tians to the south and the Hittites to the north of Palestine were the powers of the area, although neither nation was particularly menacing. Palestine was a collection of small city-states under the political control of Egypt. Egypt’s power was declining, however, and the political climate was becoming increasingly chaotic.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Just like Leviticus was almost all red, Deuteronomy is almost all green, as we read the record of Moses’ farewell message to Israel. There are no blue sections at all in this book, and God speaks only five times—all in the later part of the book.

Of the five times God speaks, the first two and the last two are directed towards Moses. In them God speaks of Moses’ imminent death. God’s words paint a rather bleak prospect of the future for the people of Israel (see especially God 2). In contrast, God’s middle intervention is directed to Joshua and has an encouraging, hopeful tone. He says, “Be strong and courageous, for you must bring the people of Israel into the land I swore to give them. I will be with you” (God 3). These words will be used again at the outset of the book of Joshua as a foundation for inheriting the promised land.

Of the 36 comments made by the Narrator, the longest is Narrator 6. It summarizes the whole book, saying, “This is the body of instruction that Moses presented to the Israelites. These are the laws, decrees, and regulations that Moses gave to the people of Israel when they left Egypt . . . ”

overview.title: How to read Joshua.
overview.content:
Do you have any unfulfilled dreams? Israel sure did . . . for 38 years they had paid a steep price for their lack of faith and obedience. Now the next generation has a chance at it, and they are not going to blow it! As they walk in obedience, a river stands still, walls fall, enemies are defeated, and their long-awaited dreams come true.

The book of Joshua divides into two sections. The first section is an amazing story of courage, faith, miracles, and innovative strategies to win one impossible battle after another. Fulfilling our dreams doesn’t depend on our skill, size, or resources, but on faith and obedience!

Once the enemies are routed, God’s plan continues to unfold through the second section. God doesn’t leave his people to squabble amongst themselves over who gets what. He gives them a very detailed description of the land he’d selected for each tribe, ensuring a foundation for peace and justice. Be encouraged as you read this book that even as God had a specific plan for the twelve tribes, so also he has a particular dream in mind for each of us!

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Joshua wrote it. The final section about Joshua’s funeral may have been composed by the priest Eleazar, son of Aaron.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Probably about 1390 bc.

overview.title: How does it fit in God’s big story?
overview.content: Joshua records the transition of the Israelites, from a nation of desert nomads, to the conquest of their promised land. It shows how the lands were divided among Israel’s 12 tribes and reminds the nation that they owed their existence to God.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
There is a lot of action in this book which recounts the watershed events of Israel’s history that brought them into their promised land. The Narrator (black) counts for about 64 percent of the book; God (red) for 8 percent; Joshua (green) for 16 percent; and everyone else (blue) for 12 percent.

There are twenty-one blue speaking parts in Joshua. They include both men and women, a priest and a prostitute, spies and kings, foreign ambassadors, and local plaintiffs. The two groups with the largest speaking parts are Israel’s Two and a Half Tribes (372 words) and Nine and a Half Tribes (192 words). Between them they reflect the concerns and aspirations of the whole nation of Israel. The two individuals who have the largest speaking parts are Caleb (speaks three times for a total of 246 words) and Rahab (speaks six times for a total of 231 words). In Caleb’s first (and largest) intervention he makes his famous request of Joshua: “Give me the hill country that the Lord promised me.” Similarly, Rahab in her third (and largest) intervention makes her famous request of the Israelite spies: “Now swear to me by the Lord that you will be kind to me and my family since I have helped you.” The bold faith exhibited here by Rahab earned her a place in faith’s “hall of fame” (Heb 11:31).

It is important to note that of the 18 times God speaks in this book, every single time it is a record of his words to Joshua. Every one of God’s words is a very personal and specific instruction for Joshua. The three largest ones are God 1, God 11, and God 17. Note how directly God speaks to Joshua in each of these three passages. In God 1 he commands Joshua at the outset of his new leadership role: “Lead . . . be strong and courageous . . . be strong and very courageous . . . be strong and courageous! Do not be afraid or discouraged.” In God 11 when Israel is defeated because of sin, and Joshua is despondent about their future military prospects, God commands Joshua: “Get up! . . . Get up! Command the people to purify themselves . . . ” In God 17—as Joshua nears the end of his life—the Lord urges him to complete his life-long call: “Be sure to give this land to Israel as a special possession, just as I have commanded you.”

Joshua’s two longest interventions are at the end of his life: Joshua 38 and Joshua 39. Here you find such famous statements as: “Be very careful to love the Lord your God . . . every promise of the Lord your God has come true . . . fear the Lord and serve him wholeheartedly . . . as for me and my family, we will serve the Lord.” Read these two sections carefully. Pay special attention to everything Joshua says about God’s character and actions. Note all the imperatives and instructions he gives to the people. Reflect upon how you might apply that in your life.

overview.title: How to read Judges.
Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Tradition credits the prophet Samuel, but we don’t know for sure who wrote it. The author may have been one of Samuel’s associates, perhaps another prophet.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Perhaps as many as 380 years after the events it describes—probably as a reflection on the nation’s story during one of Israel’s first kings, Saul or David (around 1000 bc). The book covers a period of about 350 years.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: To the people of Israel, to recount the stories of Israel’s heroes—called judges—and to give the nation’s history prior to the time it became a kingdom.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Fourteen different leaders are highlighted as judges of Israel. Seven of them have no speaking parts. They are: Othniel, son of Kenaz; Shamgar, son of Anath; Tola, son of Puah; Jair the Gileadite; Ibzan the Bethlehamite; Elon the Zebulunite; and Abdon, son of Hillel. The other leaders have speaking parts. They comprise the shared judgeship of Deborah and Barak together with the rule of five other leaders. Their spoken contributions are as follows (in order of appearance):

What might surprise you in the above list is the large speaking role that Jephthah has—more than either Gideon or Samson, both of whom are better known biblical figures. His statement to his enemies reflected the historical view of his day regarding the land: “So you see, it was the Lord, the God of Israel, who took away the land from the Am-o-rites and gave it to Israel. Why, then, should we give it back to you? You keep whatever your god Chemosh gives you, and we will keep whatever the Lord our God gives us” (Jephthah 4).

The longest red section is God 2. In it, God reminds the people of his past redemption, describes their current waywardness, and poses a key question: “I brought you out of Egypt into this land that I swore to give your ancestors, and I said I would never break my covenant with you . . . But you disobeyed my command. Why did you do this?” The purpose of this book illustrates again and again the consequences of disobedience. In light of such tragic consequences, God’s question reaches across the generations to us: Why would you ever disobey God?

overview.title: How to read Ruth.
overview.content:
If you’ve ever felt like an outsider, struggling to fit in, then you’ll identify with Ruth, the heroine of this book. In the culture in which she lived, Ruth had three strikes against her: At that time people honored women with children; she had none. Women were dependent on their husbands; she was a widow. Communities were close-knit; she was a foreigner. But this story shows how God helped this outsider, bringing her in and saving her from poverty and exclusion. So you’ll find hope in this book—a picture of God, who wants “outsiders” to come to him for help.

Ruth is such an exquisite love story that you won’t want to put it down once you’ve begun reading—so don’t. Take special note of the deep love that binds Ruth and Naomi, a love strengthened through suffering. Observe also God’s providence in the beautiful way in which Ruth and Boaz meet and fall in love with each other, a love that weds Jew and Gentile into the kingly line from which King David and his most famous descendant, Jesus, would be born.

Redemption—the theme permeates this book: Ruth is transformed from poverty to wealth, widow to wife, barren to fertile, and foreigner to Israelite. Boaz, a kinsman-redeemer who gives Ruth and her mother-in-law, Naomi, a new life, is also a key figure in the book of Ruth.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Jewish tradition points to Samuel, but it was likely written later by an unknown writer.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: The events probably took place during the period of the judges (1375–1050 bc). The date when the story was written down is not known, although it was most likely after 1000 bc.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: To the people of Israel, to retell an important story and perhaps to encourage the Israelites to include foreigners in their nation (see Isa 56:1-8).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
This is one of the few books in the Bible that has no red text at all. Though God does not speak a word in this book, the work of his hand is abundantly evident and his message of redemption is clearly heard through the surprising series of events that leads to the marriage of Ruth and Boaz. This is no ordinary love story, for their descendants include King David, the Davidic dynasty, and ultimately Messiah Jesus!

Though Ruth is the heroine of the book (thus her words are in green), both Naomi (her mother-in-law from her first marriage) and Boaz (who becomes her husband in her second marriage) have larger speaking parts. In Naomi 14 (her longest speaking part), Naomi is at her match-making best! It contains her instructions to Ruth to woo Boaz, offering herself to him as a marriage prospect (in Hebrew to “uncover his feet” is an idiomatic expression which means to “offer oneself romantically” to another). In Boaz 10 (his longest speaking part), Boaz responds to Ruth’s romantic advance and commits himself to make arrangements to marry her as quickly as possible! Clearly their union in marriage is the principle idea of this love story.

One more interesting point. There are several women who speak in this book: Naomi, Ruth (at times alone and at times along with Orpah), and the women of Bethlehem. There are also several men who speak in this book: Boaz, and his harvesters and foreman, his relative and witnesses. The words of all the women total 713; those of all the men only 691. In books written in antiquity women’s voices are rarely heard. The fact that God’s inspired record of this romance not only includes the perspective of women, but gives them more voice than the men in this love story is truly remarkable.

overview.title: How to read 1 Samuel.
overview.content:
In the two books ascribed to Samuel there are many colorful characters who deal with issues similar to our own. As you experience their tragedies and triumphs, their emotional highs and lows, you’ll learn more about yourself—and how God wants to work in your life. A common thread running through all these stories is God’s undying faithfulness to his people.

As you read this book, consider the impact of godly or ungodly leadership upon the destiny of the Israelites. This book sharply contrasts the life and leadership of King Saul and David. Both were anointed by God. Both accomplished some great feats. And both failed miserably, falling into grave sin. What’s the difference between these two leaders? Saul tried to cover up his sin whereas David was genuinely repentant of his.

We have all failed in life, but that doesn’t have to be the end of the story. We can adopt an attitude like David’s and turn back to God. Or, like Saul, we can live in the fear of man and never face our sin responsibly. Which road will you choose? Consider the examples laid out here: which ones do you want to imitate and which ones do you want to avoid?

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: The author is anonymous. The prophets Samuel, Nathan, and Gad all kept records (1Chr 29:29), which later may have been combined to form 1 and 2 Samuel. Some think official court historians may have chronicled these events (2Sa 8:16-17; 1Ki 4:3).

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Perhaps around 925 bc, sometime after the division of the nation into the northern and southern kingdoms in 930 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: 1 Samuel continues the history of God’s relationship with his people and connects the era when the judges ruled Israel to the time of the kings.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
How to read 2 Samuel.
overview.content:
“After what I’ve done, how could God ever love me?” David had as much to right to ask that as you or I ever will! Adultery, murder, rape, mutiny . . . David experienced much pain as a result of his poor choices. Yet, he is remembered as Israel’s greatest king and one of the most intimate worshipers of God. If there’s hope for David, then there’s hope for us!

In the first half of 2 Samuel, David consolidates his power until he finally becomes king over all the twelve tribes of Israel. The second half of the book recounts the personal and family troubles that plague David and lead to a great national tragedy. In the big picture, this book clearly shows God’s hand in human events. David rose to power because God selected him. David’s heroic exploits were possible because he followed the God who had anointed him. David’s moral failures show God’s justice and mercy in response to sin.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Like in the earlier book, the author of 2 Samuel is unknown to us. It’s possible the author compiled the records of Samuel and Nathan or Gad (see 1Ch 29:29).

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Probably sometime after the death of Solomon and the division of the kingdom—around 925 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: This book traces the history of Israel from the death of Saul to the end of David’s reign—approximately 1010–970 bc. It vividly shows the consequences of both faithfulness and disobedience.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The two books of Samuel provide an amazing biographical contrast between the first two kings of Israel: Saul and David. Note the first word we hear from each. In 1Sa Saul 1 the future king says, “Let’s go home. By now my father will be more worried about us than about the donkeys!” In contrast, David’s first words are, “What will a man get for killing this Philistine and ending his defiance of Israel? Who is this pagan Philistine anyway, that he is allowed to defy the armies of the living God?” (1Sa David 1). Whereas the former’s words were ones of defeat and giving up, the latter’s are words of courage, challenging the enemies of Israel.

Similarly, Saul’s last speech conveys failure and hopeless, fatalistic surrender. At the point of his death he says, “Take your sword and kill me before these pagan Philistines come to run me through and taunt and torture me” (1Sa Saul 96). In contrast David’s final words upon his death bed convey purpose and conviction, leaving a godly legacy to the crown prince, Solomon. He says, “I am going where everyone on earth must someday go. Take courage and be a man. Observe the requirements of the Lord your God, and follow all his ways. Keep the decrees, commands, regulations, and laws written in the Law of Moses so that you will be successful in all you do and wherever you go. If you do this, then the Lord will keep the promise he made to me . . . ” (1Ki David 7).

But perhaps the most telling contrast is how each responded at their moment of greatest failure: when their sin was exposed. Saul first tried to deny and cover up his disobedience in the time of war. Then he blamed it on others, and only when his guilt was apparent to all, confessed without remorse—seeking not the Lord’s heart but only how he might save face (1Sa 15:1-35). This unrepentant posture led Samuel to declare, “Obedience is better than sacrifice . . . So because you have rejected the command of the Lord, he has rejected you as king” (1Sa Samuel 36). On the other hand, when Nathan exposes David’s sin against Bathsheba and Uriah, he immediately and without making any excuse, confessed, “I have sinned against the Lord” (2Sa David 52). His whole-hearted repentance is met with forgiveness. Because of how he responded to God’s conviction, David is later called by God, “a man after my own heart” (Act Paul 5). As you read the story of the lives of these two men consider this: How will you respond when your sin is exposed? Will you be a Saul or a David?

overview.title: How to read 1 Kings.
overview.content:
As 1 Kings begins, King David was in the last days of his life, and Solomon was ready to become the ruler of the united kingdom of Israel. The first half of the book tells of the golden age of Solomon’s reign when he ruled with wisdom. But Narrator 69 tells us how Solomon violated the three major prohibitions of the Torah’s “Law of the King” (Deu 17:14-17). Moses had warned that the king must exercise self-control and not give away to boundless
• love for power (authoritarianism) by multiplying horses,
• love for pleasure (hedonism) by multiplying wives, and
• love for wealth (materialism) by multiplying silver and gold.

When Solomon dies, his son Rehoboam vows to follow his father’s policy of excessive taxation to accumulate even more wealth. As a result the kingdom is divided in two. The rest of this book and 2 Kings depict the reigns of the 19 kings in the northern kingdom of Israel and the 19 kings and one queen in the southern kingdom of Judah. The lives of these rulers provide us with both models to follow and mistakes to avoid.

As you read the two books of the Kings, keep an eye open for prophetic warnings—occasionally heeded, but often dismissed. Many of the well-known prophets, such as Isaiah, Jeremiah, Hosea and Amos, brought God’s Word during the time of the history recorded in 1 and 2 Kings. Look also for promises of rewards for honoring God contrasted with Israel’s neglect of and even contempt for the Lord.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: It is not known for sure, although Jewish tradition considered the prophet Jeremiah the author. Whoever wrote 1 and 2 Kings likely used preexisting documents, such as “The Book of the Acts of Solomon” (11:41); “The Book of the History of the Kings of Israel” (14:19); and “The Book of the History of the Kings of Judah” (14:29).

overview.title: When and why was it written?
overview.content: Most likely it was written during the Babylonian exile, somewhere between 560 and 550 bc. The book demonstrates the necessity of taking God’s commands and promises seriously.

overview.title: What period of history does it cover?
overview.content: This book covers the last days of David, the reign and fall of Solomon, and the division of the kingdom—approximately the years from 971 to 846 bc.

overview.title: Why are there two books of Kings?
overview.content: Originally, the two books were one. Primarily because of its length, Kings was divided into two separate books for easier handling. In fact, together with 1 and 2 Samuel they form a continuous four-volume history of the nation of Israel.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Consider all the words of the Jewish monarchs (the green parts) of 1 Samuel through 2 Kings. There are three groupings of kings. First we have the three kings of the united kingdom (1050–930 bc). Consider the words of Saul (17 percent), David (61 percent), and Solomon (22 percent). David is clearly the star and his longest speech is David 120 in 2 Samuel (repeated in Psalm 18). This worship of God for his amazing deliverance is the heart of this multi-generational story.

Second, the northern kingdom of Israel (930–722 bc) is ruled by 19 kings. We have no sayings from thirteen of them: Nadab, Baasha, Elah, Zimri, Omri, Jehoahaz, Jeroboam II, Zechariah, Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, Pekah, and Hoshea. Imagine that: nothing they said was worth recording for future generations! The remaining six kings with speaking parts are: Jeroboam I, Ahab, Ahaziah, Joram, Jehu, and Jehoash. Of these Jehu (841-814 bc) is the one who has the most words recorded (36 percent of the words of the northern kings). In his longest spoken part Jehu defends the just execution of King Joram (2Ki Jehu of Israel 8). Jehu is the only northern king who is commended by the Lord: “You have done well” (2Ki God 3).

Third, the southern kingdom of Judah (930–586 bc) is ruled by 19 kings and 1 queen. Eleven of them have no words recorded for posterity: Abijah/Abijam, Jehoram/Joram, Ahaziah, Uzziah/Azariah, Jotham, Ma-nas-she, Amon, Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, and Zedekiah. The nine monarchs with speaking parts include six good kings (Asa, Jehoshaphat, Joash/Jehoash, Amaziah, Hezekiah, Josiah), two bad kings (Rehoboam, Ahaz), and a bad queen (Athaliah). The southern monarch with the most spoken words is Hezekiah (715–686 bc). His longest part is his history-making prayer which is recorded both in 2 Kings (Hezekiah of Judah 3) and in Isaiah (Hezekiah of Judah 2). His prayer concludes, “Now, O Lord our God, rescue us . . . then all the kingdoms of the earth will know that you alone, O Lord, are God.”

overview.title: How to read 2 Kings.
overview.content:
The four-volume story that begins with 1 Samuel and ends with 2 Kings is all about leadership and its impact on a nation. The leaders that shape history are not only the kings and their entourage, but also the prophets and their disciples. In fact, the spoken words of the prophets (a total of 5,126) in 1 and 2 Kings exceeds those of the kings themselves (a total of 4,825). The point? History is shaped not only by political decisions but by how carefully we heed the words of God spoken through his messengers.

Note that the book begins with an extended emphasis on two prophets: Elijah, whose ministry ends early in the book; and Elisha, who calls people back to the worship of the one true God. A number of other prophets, some of whom wrote Bible books—like Amos, Hosea, Obadiah, and Isaiah—also ministered during this time. The rest of 2 Kings is an account of the acts of various kings of Israel and Judah, culminating in the destruction of the northern kingdom of Israel and the deportation of the southern kingdom of Judah into exile. As you scrutinize this book, take note of the kind of attitudes and behaviors that pleased God and the kinds that grieved him.

This book bluntly states the tragic consequences of idolatry: Israel’s defeat and captivity in Assyria, and Judah’s defeat and captivity in Babylon. Also pay special attention to the summary statements (17:7-23; 24:8-20) that detail the terrible losses God’s people suffered because of their sin. But this story is told not just to inform us about the past but to warn us today. The same principles apply now as then: God always holds up the righ-teous standard, lovingly pursues people, is ready to forgive, but will bring judgment—though he prefers mercy—to those who persist in disobedience.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: We can’t be sure. According to tradition, one or more prophets compiled this multi-volume work. Whoever wrote it made use of historical sources that had been written during this time (see 1Ki 11:41; 14:19, 29).

overview.title: When was this book written?
overview.content: 2 Kings covers events between 853 and 562 bc. Originally, the book was part of 1 Kings and was probably written after the fall of Jerusalem in 586 bc, but before 550 bc.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: Originally it was written to preserve the history of Israel and Judah for the Jews living in exile in Babylon. The author wanted his readers to remember the consequences of unfaithfulness and to learn from their mistakes!

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
There are 21 speaking prophets in the four-volume history recorded in1Sa–2Ki. They include not only true but also some false prophets, some famous and others nameless, both old and young, male and female. They are listed here in order of appearance:
• Samuel the prophet (51 times in 1Sa)
• Gad the prophet (1 time in 1Sa; 2 times in 2Sa)
• Nathan the prophet (4 times in 2Sa; 2 times in 1Ki)
• Ahijah the prophet (4 times in 1Ki)
• A prophet from Judah (7 times in 1Ki)
• An old prophet of Bethel (10 times in 1Ki)
• Elijah the prophet (34 times in 1Ki; 9 times in 2Ki)
• The prophets of Baal (1 time in 1Ki)
• Elisha the prophet (1 time in 1Ki; 77 times in 2Ki)
• A prophet to Ahab (10 times in 1Ki)
• Ahab’s false prophets (3 times in 1Ki)
• Zedekiah son of Kenaanah (2 times in 1Ki)
• Micaiah the prophet (7 times in 1Ki)
• Some prophets of Bethel (1 time in 2Ki)
• Some prophets of Jericho (4 times in 2Ki)
• Some prophets of Gilgal (1 time in 2Ki)
• Some prophets by Jordan (2 times in 2Ki)
• A prophet in need (2 times in 2Ki)
• A young prophet (3 times in 2Ki)
• Isaiah the prophet (9 times in 2Ki)
• Huldah the prophetess (1 time in 2Ki)

overview.title: How to read 1 Chronicles.
overview.content:
When you find your old graduation program or school yearbook, whose name or face do you look for first? Chances are, it’s your own—or at least those of your closest friends. There is a universal human need to belong. The Israelites were no different. The books of Chronicles demonstrated to the Israelites how they fit into God’s plan. These books still show us the principles of how to belong to God.

The first part of 1 Chronicles reads like a family tree; beginning with Adam, it is a list of all who formed links in the story of God’s chosen family. After a brief chapter on the reign of King Saul (chapter 10), the rest of the book is a record of what David did as king of all Israel, ending with an account of David’s death. Whereas the parallel accounts of Samuel and Kings highlighted the interaction between the kings and the prophets, the two books of Chronicles emphasize the role of the priests and the place of the temple. As you read 1 Chronicles, consider how the returning exiles would have responded to the message of this book. What does it have to say to you about God’s faithfulness?

As you read the names (chapters 1-9), reflect on how this genealogy connected Israelites who had just returned from exile with their ancestors and the promises God had given to their nation. Also, watch for David’s role in leading Israel to worship God, and look for ways to enhance your own worship.

overview.title: Who wrote it?
overview.content: Traditionally, it’s been thought that Ezra wrote Chronicles. But an unknown priest or Levite may have been its author.

overview.title: When and why was it written?
overview.content: Chronicles was written around 450–400 bc—more than six centuries after the first events it records, about 100-150 years after the last events in 2 Chronicles 36. The two books (originally one) reexamine the history of Israel. The writer’s goal was that those Israelites too would see their link to godly roots and rediscover their heritage.

overview.title: What period of history does it cover?
overview.content: 1 Chronicles looks at the genealogical records from the beginning of recorded history until after the Babylonian exile. From chapter 11 on, it focuses on the reign of King David.

overview.title: Why repeat material from 2 Samuel and 2 Kings?
overview.content: Chronicles is not a rehash of other Old Testament texts. Samuel and Kings were written to a people in exile, who wondered how and why they got there. Chronicles was written to a people returned from exile, who wondered if they still fit into God’s plan.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
More than four-fifths of all the words in this book are those of the Narrator. As you begin the book, page after page is printed in black alone. But sheer volume alone does not tell the whole story. Nestled within the first 6,329 words of the text are the 27 blue words that comprise the prayer of a man desperate to clarify his identity and establish his inheritance. Jabez cried out to God, “Oh, that you would bless me and expand my territory! Please be with me in all that I do, and keep me from all trouble and pain!” In a very real way Jabez’ prayer reflected the cry of every exile returning from the trouble and pain of the captivity. They were dissatisfied with the status quo of the past and longed for God’s presence and blessing to give them a God-intended inheritance.

The SourceView format allows for the prayer to stand out as the Narrator intended. As this God-inspired text reviews myriads of generations of people, only Jabez is highlighted. Clearly God was delighted with this prayer. He was drawn to this man who wanted to shake off mediocrity and presses in for God’s maximum. Therefore the Narrator comments, “And God granted him his request.” As you read this book set your heart to be like Jabez: be passionate to live for God’s highest for your life!

overview.title: How to read 2 Chronicles.
overview.content:
In 1 Chronicles, the first break in the story of the Narrator is made by Jabez’ prayer. Similarly, in 2 Chronicles, the first time the Narrator pauses the opening issue once again is prayer. On this occasion God asks Solomon, “What do you want? Ask, and I will give it to you!” (God 1) Solomon’s prayer is a godly request for wisdom. However, the final time God speaks to Solomon (God 3), he warns him, “If you or your descendants abandon me and disobey the decrees and commands I have given you, and if you serve and worship other gods, then I will uproot the people from this land that I have given them.” This unfortunately is the sad end of this story. As you read this book remember: it’s not how well you begin the race, but how well you end it that counts!

As select stories of the monarchs are recounted, a fascinating study in leadership success and failure emerges. Seek to emulate the character qualities of those kings who did “what was good in the Lord’s sight” and avoid the example of those who did “what was evil in the Lord’s sight.” This is history from God’s vantage point! The central principle on display here is that “the eyes of the Lord search the whole earth in order to strengthen those whose hearts are fully committed to him” (Hanani the Prophet 1). To pursue God is wisdom; to abandon him is foolishness.

This is true not only for individual leaders, but also for a whole people. A nation that honors God will see success; one that does not will experience devastation. Focusing mainly on the southern kingdom of Judah, the writer tells about the temple, the priesthood, God’s covenant, and the kings to reinforce his central theme. By the end of the book you’ll have read about the downfall of the nation—the result of rulers and people straying from God.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Possibly Ezra, a priest and scribe who also wrote the book that bears his name. Some suggest that an unknown priest or Levite may have been its author.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Approximately 450–400 bc, although this book covers the period from Solomon’s reign (about 970 bc) to the fall of Jerusalem (586 bc).

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: To those who resettled in Judah and were rebuilding their society. Studying the early, prosperous days of their nation reinforced their faith in God’s promises. God also wanted them to learn from their mistakes so they would not repeat the past.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: After the Babylonians destroyed Jerusalem, the Jews were taken as captives to Babylon where they lived for seventy years. After the Persians conquered Babylon, several groups of Jews were allowed to return to their homeland.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content: A comparison of the story told in 1Sa–2Ki with 1Ch–2Ch reveals some interesting items. Four kings of Israel who have speaking parts in 1Sa–2Ki (Jeroboam I, Ahaziah, Joram, and Jehu) do not have speaking parts in 1Ch–2Ch. Conversely one king of Judah who does not have a speaking part in 1Sa–2Ki (Abijah) does have a speaking part in 1Ch–2Ch. Note the number of words each monarch speaks in these two accounts:

overview.title: How to read Ezra.
overview.content:
To get the most out of this book it is important to understand the major political shifts that were going on internationally. In 539 bc the Babylonian empire fell to the Medo-Persians, who embraced a new policy favorable to the Jews who had been living as refugees in exile from their homeland. This book tells of how the exiles returned to rebuild Jerusalem and its temple. Ezra reveals God as the power behind earthly events, moving even pagan kings according to his purposes (1:1; 6:22; 7:6, 27).

It’s helpful to be aware that while the book is generally laid out in a chronological sequence, chapter 4 is a parenthetical summary of the political debate of international leaders around the fate of Jerusalem. If you want to read the book chronologically you would read it in this order:
• 539 bc      1:1-2:69
• 537 bc      2:70-3:7
• 535 bc      3:8-4:5, 24
• 520 bc      5:1-6:12
• 516 bc      6:13-22
• 486 bc      4:6
• 458 bc      7:1-10:44
• 444 bc      4:7-23

As you read Ezra, you will observe several characteristics of God that will give you courage in your own life: He is a forgiving God, who gives new opportunities to respond to him in faith and in service. He is a caring God, who will accomplish his will in spite of opposition and who will offer his protection. He is the God above all gods, the one true God who expects your genuine worship.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Some think Ezra wrote it along with the book of Nehemiah. Many have concluded that the author of Ezra and Nehemiah also wrote 1 and 2 Chronicles.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Ezra and Nehemiah were linked together as a single book in the original Hebrew Bible. The events in Nehemiah were completed sometime after 440 bc.

overview.title: What historical events surrounded it?
overview.content: Good news! After years of living in exile, the Jews saw Cyrus the Persian (539 bc) conquer Babylon and allow the exiles to return to Jerusalem to rebuild the temple.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Of the 15 blue sections in Ezra, seven of them are transcriptions of official documents—letters and decrees—that shaped the socio-political environment in which Jerusalem and its temple were rebuilt. Two letters were sent to the Persian monarchs by enemies of the Jews seeking to impede the restoration of Jerusalem. One was written by Tattenai and a group of fellow leaders, and the other was written by Rehum and Shimshai. Other letters of opposition are spoken about (4:6-7), but we do not have a record of their full text.

In response we have the record of five documents from the Persian monarchs. Two by Cyrus II of Persia in favor of the Jews were an extraordinary fulfillment of the prophecy in God 77 recorded in Isaiah. These were followed by another pro-Jewish decree by Darius I of Persia. Finally we have two decrees by Artaxerxes I of Persia. In the first his policy is swayed by the Jewish opposition, but then he reverses himself in the second decree and gives full support to the restoration of Jerusalem, including substantial financial assistance. His is the final word on the subject. He encourages Ezra: “Teach the law to anyone who does not know it. Anyone who refuses to obey the law of your God and the law of the king will be punished immediately, either by death, banishment, confiscation of goods, or imprisonment.”

Of the four green sections, Ezra’s prayer of confession (Ezra 2) is by far the longest. As you read it consider the many rich insights it has into the character of God. You may want to make a list of all the truths this amazing prayer contains about God and his ways.

overview.title: How to read Nehemiah.
overview.content:
If you’ve ever faced an overwhelming task or felt inadequate to meet a challenge, you’ll identify with Nehemiah. He understood the timeless issues of leadership: motivating others to action, overcoming criticism, and not succumbing to frustration, to name a few. Addressing both the practical and spiritual aspects of leadership, Nehemiah shows how to tackle God’s difficult assignments and overcome both opposition and apathy.

Nehemiah’s story can give anyone hope, as an exiled Jew becomes governor of a disspirited remnant and a city in ruins. In just 51 days they accomplish the impossible: they rebuild city’s wall and restore its gates. After rebuilding Jerusalem’s wall (chapters 1-6), Nehemiah gave himself to restoring the people of God (chapters 7-12). His obedience to God shaped every area of society.

Watch as Nehemiah combines profound spirituality with very practical, down-to-earth action. We clearly see this when we are told, “But we prayed to our God and guarded the city day and night to protect ourselves” (The Narrator 16).

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Probably the writer of Ezra (which was originally combined with Nehemiah), who many scholars think also wrote 1 and 2 Chronicles. He likely drew from Nehemiah’s memoirs and from census records. The incidents recorded here occurred between 444 and 432 bc. The book was probably compiled about 430 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To remind God’s people of their spiritual heritage and to keep them from becoming careless toward the Lord.

overview.title: What historical events surrounded it?
overview.content: The Babylonians conquered Judah in 586 bc. When Persia conquered Babylon (539 bc), the Jews were permitted to return to Jerusalem. By 445 bc, however, the challenges of rebuilding their homeland had demoralized them. Under Ezra, they had rebuilt the temple, but the walls of the city remained in rubble.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
As you read through Nehemiah’s words (in green) note that fully one third of them are prayers—including not only his first (and longest) statement as well as his last statement in the book, but many others as well (see Nehemiah 1,7,14,18,22,26,29,30). Similarly, the longest blue section in the book is a prayer (The Levite Leaders 2). There are frequent references to prayer, praise, and worship (see The Narrator 2,6,15,16,30,38,39,42,43,46 and The Levite Leaders 1).

Related to prayer is the theme of spiritual warfare. As you read through the book note the frequent opposition that Nehemiah faces and how he confronts it. Pay attention to the accusations launched by Tobiah, Sanballat, and others (in blue). Are there similarities between their words and accusations you have had to face? What can you learn from Nehemiah’s response to them? How can this equip you to overcome the powers of darkness that seek to war against you and the rebuilding of your life?

There is an interesting grammatical interchange within the text of the Narrator. Though much of it is in the first person (Nehemiah is framing his own story, using first person pronouns like “I, me, my”), there are other parts in which Nehemiah is referred to in the third person by the Narrator (see Neh 8:9,10; 12:26,47). Some of this may be because Nehemiah is integrating other documents into the text (Neh 7:5) and/or because he was not always present (Neh 13:6). Whatever the cause, the Narrator uses different voices to refer to Nehemiah as noted below:
• Nehemiah in First Person 1:1-7:5 (The Narrator 1-38)
• Nehemiah in Third Person 7:6-12:30 (The Narrator 38-46)
• Nehemiah in First Person 12:31-43 (The Narrator 46)
• Nehemiah in Third Person 12:44-13:5 (The Narrator 46)
• Nehemiah in First Person 13:6-31 (The Narrator 46-55)

overview.title: How to read Esther.
overview.content:
It’s the stuff of fairy tales. A poor orphaned refugee wins a beauty pageant and rises to become queen of the land. When her people are threatened with destruction by a vile enemy, she puts her life on the line for them. Then, in a surprising twist of circumstances the tables are turned on her enemies and what seemed like certain doom is transformed into an incredible victory! The amazing thing is that it’s no fairy tale: it’s all true!

This book is so packed with suspense that you will find it hard to put down once you begin reading. Expertly crafted character profiles of people like Esther, Mordecai, Haman, and King Xerxes I of Persia make for a gripping read. But this is more than just great literature. It can be a transformational read! As you consider the strengths and weaknesses of these people you can glean important lessons for your own character growth. Even as Mordecai challenged Esther to consider why God had raised her up “for just such a time as this” (4:14), reflect on the same question: Why has God allowed you to be alive today? What challenges does he want you to take on? What redemptive acts does he want to accomplish through you?

Although God’s name is not mentioned once in the book of Esther, his influence permeates the whole narrative. Pay attention to the subtle way this book reveals God’s character and accents his faithfulness. Notice the indirect allusions to God’s involvement in the life of his people. Take comfort that he is in charge, even when things seem out of control.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: The author is unknown, but it is clear from details throughout that the author was a devout Jew.

overview.title: Where and when was it written?
overview.content: In Persia, sometime between 460 and 350 bc. Esther became queen in 479 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: As a history, to record the events leading to the establishment of the Jewish observance of Purim (9:24-32), and as a dramatic reminder to the Jews of God’s protection.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
There are no red passages in Esther. The Narrator (black) occupies 73 percent of the text. The other 27 percent are comprised by the words of Esther (in green) and the other eight speaking parts (in blue) in this dramatic short story. The cast is listed here, ranked by the number of words spoken by each:
• Esther
• Xerxes I of Persia
• Haman
• Memucan the Adviser
• Xerxes’ Attendants
• Haman’s Wife & Friends
• Mordecai
• Harbona the Eunuch
• Xerxes’ Palace Officials

Xerxes asks 14 of the 18 questions found in the book. Only one is directed to Haman:
• What should I do to honor a man who truly pleases me? (Xerxes I of Persia 10)
• What must be done to Queen Vashti? (Xerxes I of Persia 1)
• What penalty does the law provide for a queen who refuses to obey the king’s orders, properly sent through his eunuchs? (Xerxes I of Persia 2)
• What reward or recognition did we ever give Mordecai for this? (Xerxes I of Persia 7)
• Who is that in the outer court? (Xerxes I of Persia 8)
• Will he even assault the queen right here in the palace, before my very eyes? (Xerxes I of Persia 16)
• What do you want, Queen Esther? (Xerxes I of Persia 4)
• What is your request? (Xerxes I of Persia 4)
• What is your request? (Xerxes I of Persia 6)
• What is your request? (Xerxes I of Persia 13)
• Who would do such a thing? (Xerxes I of Persia 14)
• Who would be so presumptuous as to touch you? (Xerxes I of Persia 15)
• If they have done that here, what has happened in the rest of the provinces? (Xerxes I of Persia 19)
• But now, what more do you want? (Xerxes I of Persia 19)

overview.title: How to read Job.
overview.content:
At one time or another we all wrestle with life’s most difficult questions: Why is there evil and suffering in the world? Why do the righ-teous suffer? Where is God when it hurts? Simplistic answers gloss over reality and leave us with serious questions about God’s character. You will find none of that here. Job’s agonized search for real answers amidst terrible pain leads to a transformational encounter with God. His dramatic experience brings us face-to-face with the God who holds the answers to our most profound questions.

The beginning of Job gives the reader an important “behind the scenes” glimpse into the heavenly debate that set the framework for the earthly tragedies that befell Job. We hear Satan accuse God. He declares that God’s kingdom is like his own, built on self-serving interests and that the only reason that Job serves God is because he derives benefit from God’s many blessings. He essentially tells God, “Take away the blessings and Job will no longer love you.” The gauntlet is thrown down and the stage is set for a cosmic challenge, the result of which will be determined on earth. All heaven holds its breath as Satan—not God—takes away the good things in Job’s life. What would happen? How would Job respond? Imagine the jubilant sound of victory when the heavenly hosts hear Job, bereft of blessings—having lost property, cattle and children—proclaim in the midst of his agony, “Praise the name of the Lord!” (Job 2). Job demonstrated that he loved God simply for who God is and not for what Job had received from him. God had been vindicated and Satan was humiliated.

But only we—the readers—have the benefit of this “behind the scenes” perspective. All Job knew was that all of a sudden everything good in his life was destroyed. He did not understand why. He was desperate for understanding. He wanted an explanation. He asked myriads of questions until finally he met God afresh amidst the pain. That encounter with God changed everything. At the climactic finalé of the drama, Job exclaimed, “I had only heard about you before, but now I have seen you with my own eyes” (Job 16). Ask God that you too may see God afresh as you read Job’s story.

Consider the various voices heard throughout the book—the same ones we encounter in times of trial—God, the devil, well-meaning friends, family, and Job’s own heart. Savor the wide range of literary techniques used in this book: dialogue, poetry, proverbs, riddles, laments, curses, and metaphors. Read carefully, remembering that we have the privilege of seeing the “behind the scenes” reality that Job and his friends did not.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: The author is unknown. The book may well be the most ancient document in the Bible, dating back to the times of the early Hebrew patriarchs in the second millennium bc. Many think the story of Job was passed down orally from generation to generation and only later put into writing.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To address the question of suffering. The writer tells Job’s story in a way that allows readers to identify with his spiritual and philosophical struggles. It points us to a good God who ultimately vindicates those who love him.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
This book is written as a drama. Job’s words (in green) comprise 48 percent of the story; God’s words (in red) 12 percent; the words of the supporting cast (in blue) 34 percent; and those of the Narrator (in black) a mere 6 percent. As you read, it’s important to pay special attention to who is speaking. At the end of the book God says to Eliphaz the Temanite, “I am angry with you and your two friends, for you have not spoken accurately about me, as my servant Job has . . . [and once again!] you have not spoken accurately about me, as my servant Job has” (God 12). So be careful! Even though some of the declarations of Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar seem quite “orthodox,” they are rejected by God and therefore their line of reasoning should be rejected by us as well.

Because this book addresses the question of suffering in the world, question-asking is central to the story line. Note that all the key speakers except one—Satan—ask questions. This list ranks the speakers by the number of questions each one asks:
• Job
• God
• Eliphaz the Temanite
• Elihu the Buzite
• Bildad the Shuhite
• Zophar the Naamathite
• Job’s Wife

When God shows up mid story (Narrator 42), he does not answer Job’s many questions one by one. Instead he does the opposite of what Job’s friends had done. They had attempted to dismiss or argue away his questions. It seems that God realizes that Job has about come to the end of his question-asking, and comes alongside him to encourage him in the process by adding more questions to the list. God thus leads him onward in a process of discovery that takes him beyond his pain and into a place of fresh revelation about God. May God do the same for you as you contemplate the questions raised in Job.

overview.title: How to read Psalms.
overview.content:
This book can give voice to your deepest feelings. Whatever you feel—joy or sorrow, peace or anguish, hope or despair—you’ll find your feelings expressed somewhere in Psalms. The words written in this ancient hymn book are so relevant for today’s joys and challenges that you may think they were written just to address your current situation. Some of these poetic prayers are directed heavenward, filled with grateful praise, repentant confessions, or intercessory petitions. Others are directed outward, imparting practical wisdom and godly counsel to the hearer. Yet others—like Psalm 103—are directed inward, as the psalmist wrestles to align his soul with a God-oriented perspective of life. Amidst this great diversity, you’re sure to find a psalm that relates to the circumstances you are facing this day.

Your commitment to spend time in this book will be abundantly rewarded. As you read and reread each psalm, new layers of meaning will be unfolded for your immediate and long-term benefit. Bear in mind as you read, that Hebrew poetry is different from most other poetry. The psalmists employed conceptual parallelism, where thoughts rather than sounds are rhymed in tones of harmonious echoes or clarifying contrasts. This produces rich material that you can linger over for hours as you meditate on a passage, extracting from it life-giving insights.

Be aware that the psalms are poetry, not doctrinal essays. Their writers usually were more interested in describing their heart-felt emotions, not in expositing theological truth. Think of the psalms as entries in a diary—they reflect people’s most intimate dealings with God. Watch for vivid figures of speech and passionate honesty. Poetic language calls for you to read with your heart as well as with your mind, making application in all areas of your life.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: David—Israel’s most celebrated king—is said to have authored 73 of the psalms. Though the authorship of fifty psalms is unknown, the remaining psalms were written by Asaph, Korah’s descendants, Solomon, Moses, and Ethan the Ezrahite.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: Some authors originally may have intended their psalms for solitary singing or meditation. Others meant their psalms to be used in group worship. As their value for spiritual encouragement was plain to see, the psalms were collected as helps for times of praise, prayer, and instruction about God.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
This is the one book in the Bible where chapter divisions reflect the design of the original text. Because each psalm is a unique literary piece in this devotional anthology, the current way we refer to them reflects ancient usage. We see this in the book of Acts: Paul, preaching in Antioch of Pisidia, quotes from “what the second psalm says about Jesus” (Act 13:33). What was the second psalm then is still the second psalm today.

The psalms are the personal prayers and reflections of individuals facing the varied circumstances of life. As such they are not a dramatic narrative like the rest of the Old Testament. For this reason, the colored text of the SourceView format simply serves to highlight the authorship of each psalm. Black is used for the text of the fifty psalms that do not specify who is their author (black is also used for the descriptive titles of each psalm and for any musical instructions inserted for the use of the choir director). Green is used for the text of the 73 psalms written by David, as he is the lead psalmist in the Bible. Blue is used for all the psalms with other authors. These include twelve psalms by Asaph (50, 73-83), eleven by Korah’s descendants (42,44-49,84-85,87-88), two by Solomon (72,127), and one each by Moses (90) and Ethan the Ezrahite (89).

An interesting note on authorship occurs when one reads the words of the Believers in Jerusalem 2 (Act 4:25-26). Gathered to pray after Peter and John had been threatened by the Jewish leadership council, they noted that God “spoke long ago by the Holy Spirit through our ancestor David” and went on to quote Psa 2:1-2. So, they believed that David was the author of the second psalm, even though the text does not specify the authorship of this particular psalm.

overview.title: How to read Proverbs.
overview.content:
Giving advice is big business. From daytime talk shows to advice columns and self-help books, our culture is glutted with knowledge—but often starved for wisdom. From managing money to the dangers of adultery to identifying true friends, Proverbs offers spiritual depth and practical help—wisdom that works and insights that won’t wear out.

A proverb is a truth condensed into a few words, stated in a memorable fashion, to be applied in a variety of life situations. This collection paints vivid pictures—contrasting issues such as foolishness and wisdom, laziness and diligence, adultery and faithfulness, unjust use of finances and godly stewardship, right and wrong use of the tongue, true and false friendships, etc. Look for principles of building strong marriages, parenting, and relationships on the job. Pay close attention to the differing outcomes of those who follow God’s wisdom and those who don’t. Remember that these proverbs aren’t rigid rules, but godly descriptions of how life usually works.

Careful study of this book will reap rich dividends. You will grow in wisdom as you choose to live by Proverbs. Because it is an anthology of distinct, pithy sayings, you are not meant to read it like other books—from beginning to end in a sitting or two. It requires slow, thoughtful meditation. You may want to consider reading a chapter a day for a month to give yourself time to reflect, and more importantly, time to implement the wisdom you’ll find.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: King Solomon wrote much of it. Agur son of Jakeh, King Lemuel’s mother, and other wise individuals also contributed to this compilation of godly wisdom.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Solomon reigned during the tenth century bc, but many of the proverbs were not compiled and edited until several centuries later—probably around 715–686 bc.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: The people of Israel, particularly to the young people as they were starting out on life’s journey, to show them how godly wisdom merges with real life.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The first nine chapters lay a foundation for applied wisdom. The purpose of these proverbs “is to teach people to live disciplined and successful lives, to help them do what is right, just, and fair. These proverbs will give insight to the simple, knowledge and discernment to the young” (Narrator 1). In the dialogue that ensues, wisdom is personified and speaks directly to the life of the wayward, the naive, the foolish, the young, the lost. These words are in red for they are in fact the words of God, intent on leading us into the way of life. Read and reread these red sections. Pay special attention to the imperatives and the promises contained in these passages.

In chapters 10-29 almost every verse is a stand-alone proverb. You could read them in almost any order without altering the meaning of the text. This forms the main collection of sayings in the book and begins with an extended list of proverbs authored or compiled by Solomon (in green), followed by two smaller lists edited by “the wise” (in blue) and concludes with another list created by Solomon.

Finally the last two chapters contain the instructions of Agur son of Jakeh, and King Lemuel’s mother.

overview.title: How to read Ecclesiastes.
overview.content:
Searching for life’s meaning? Wrestling with the big questions? Ecclesiastes will rock your world. “The Teacher” had all the time, money, and intellectual capacity to come up with the big answers. This book describes the author’s search with painful transparency, honest confessions of doubts, struggles with faith, and acknowledgments of disillusionment. But what he found is that—apart from God—there is no meaning to life. Wealth and comfort don’t create a sense of fulfillment. Food and pleasure leave you empty. Vocational success and intellectual accomplishments don’t fill the void. Even when these and all other desires are fulfilled, life is bleak if lived apart from God.

Ecclesiastes challenges easy conclusions and dares to face hard questions. It shows the meaninglessness of a pleasure-seeking world. It offers insight into the life of a seeker who has yet to find God. In so doing, Ecclesiastes’ description of the hopelessness of life without God creates a dissatisfaction with living the status quo. Certainly there is more! Clues to how to discover this something more are found in the prologue (1:1-11) and the epilogue (12:9-14). These passages frame the author’s internal debate and point the seeker to the only ultimate solution: life lived with God. In a very real sense, Ecclesiastes prepares us to hear the gospel message. God provided the answer to the emptiness of life through his son Jesus, who came that we might live a meaningful and abundant life!

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: According to tradition Solomon is the author. However, some think it may well have been written for Solomon and not by him. Perhaps some insightful court minstrel wrote it, identifying with the king, artfully hoping to call Solomon back to a life with God and away from the meaningless pursuits that had led his heart astray.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Probably during the years of Solomon’s reign, from 970 to 930 bc.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: Ecclesiastes is a study in competing worldviews, perspectives, and philosophies. It is designed to teach wisdom by showing that a life lived without God at its core is meaningless. After looking at all of life’s issues from many angles, the author summarizes: “That’s the whole story. Here now is my final conclusion: Fear God and obey his commands, for this is everyone’s duty” (Ecc 12:13).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The different SourceView colors used in this book clarify the dialogue going on within the author’s head. This is an internal drama. Both the black text of the Narrator and the green text of the Teacher are the debating voices of his personal soliloquy as he seeks to discover life’s meaning. Even six of the seven voices that are represented by the blue text (Some People 1-5 and Some Man 1) are simply the proverbial “they”—nothing more than a dramatic foil to move the debate forward inside the Teacher’s own head.

The seventh and final blue text occurs in the concluding section of the book, when the Teacher turns outward and begins exhorting the youth around him. He speaks directly to his readers and exhorts them with several pointed imperatives. Then he draws them (us!) in by involving the readers in the discussion: “Don’t let the excitement of youth cause you to forget your Creator. Honor him in your youth before you grow old and say, ‘Life is not pleasant anymore’ ” (The Narrator 18—The Reader 1).

As you read this book it may help you by focusing on the imperatives. Writing down all the commands contained in the book will give you an overall framework that will help you understand and apply its message.

overview.title: How to read Song of Songs.
overview.content:
Sex . . . in the Bible? Yes! For some it is surprising to find a poem of love and sexual intimacy included in the Bible. What should we make of it? Some suggest that we should read it only symbolically, as a picture of God’s unconditional love for his people. Others suggest that it is best understood as a marriage handbook and find a treasure of marital delights in it. Perhaps there is value in both approaches. At any rate, Song of Songs is a beautiful picture of the physical side of love, and its appropriate expression within the bonds of marriage as part of God’s wonderful creation.

As you read this book, it may help you to understand that the images of beauty, charm, and elegance contained within its pages are to be enjoyed intuitively, not analytically. If you are married, you may find encouragement here to reflect on the level of intimacy and care that characterizes your relationship with your spouse. If you are single, you may take to heart the final exhortation to chastity until marriage (SoS 8:8-9). Song of Songs celebrates romantic, sexual love within the context of marriage as God’s gift. Reserving oneself sexually before marriage and joyful fidelity within marriage are the intended results of this beautiful song: “My lover is mine, and I am his” (The Bride 7).

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: The Hebrew preposition in the opening title can by translated “of”—that is why this song is traditionally seen as being “of Solomon,” of his authorship. But this Hebrew preposition may equally be translated as “for”—in this case this dramatic poem would have been composed not by Solomon but for him. Given Solomon’s failures in the area of marital love, this second view may be more likely. This song may well have been composed by an anonymous court entertainer for Solomon to be performed on the occasion of one of his many marriage feasts.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: During King Solomon’s reign from 970 to 930 b.c. We know that by the end of his life Solomon had acquired 700 wives and 300 concubines (1Ki 11:3). Perhaps this song was composed at some point in Solomon’s reign in hopes to arrest his unfettered multiplication of wives.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content:
