If an unknown court musician was the author of this song, he may have written it for Solomon in an attempt to remind him that the best romantic love is that which exists between one man and one woman in exclusive marital commitment. For this reason the author celebrates the wonder of committed, singular love between two people for life (SoS 2:16; 3:3; 4:12; 6:2,9; 7:10,13; 8:10). It appears that the author may have wanted to call Solomon back to earlier, wiser days in which he had exercised self-control over his amorous passions.

Unfortunately, it seems that Solomon did not heed the artful exhortation of this song and proceeded to multiply not only wives (1Ki 11:1-13), but also gold (1Ki 10:14-25) and horses (1Ki 10:26-29). He succumbed to an uncontrolled lust for sex, money, and power, having failed to obey the Law of the King (Deu 17:16-17). Instead of walking in the purity, generosity, and servanthood that God expects from godly leaders, he fell into hedonism, materialism, and authoritarianism. The author of this song may well have seen where Solomon was heading and tried to call him back to God’s standard of romantic love with this beautiful, poetic ode.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
This book follows the patterns of ancient dramatic works. Don’t think of a modern-day theatrical production, but rather of a ballad to be sung or recited by soloists and a choir. Changing voices, especially those of the choir, served as artistic clues to the attentive audience that there was a scene change. Several opening scenes—from the initial words of The Choir 1 through the exhortation of The Bridegroom 6—describe the building romance of a pre-nuptial relationship.

The Choir’s major piece (The Choir 5) introduces the major shift in the young couple’s relationship. The choir here sings of the marriage celebration, and then the lovers speak of their love for one another on their honeymoon night (The Bride 8—The Bridegroom 8). The Choir concludes this scene with a call to delight in love (The Choir 6).

From the words of The Bride 10 to the end of the book, various scenes describe the growing love between husband and wife as they learn to resolve conflict and deepen their intimacy with one another.

The Choir has 11 interventions (12 percent of the words) of the song. The Bride is considered the lead character because she speaks 17 times and her words comprise 51 percent of the song. The Bridegroom plays the supporting role, speaking 13 times with 37 percent of the words. This simple layout would have been revolutionary in Solomon’s day—a day in which the words of the king were of paramount importance and the words of one of his consorts would have amounted to very little indeed. The unknown author is exalting the role of the woman in the marital relationship in a way that would have been socially transformational.

SourceView helps us see the song’s highlights. The Bridegroom’s longest part is that which comes on his wedding night as he extols the beauty of his new wife (The Bridegroom 7). The Bride’s longest part comes early in their married state when they have a misunderstanding and find themselves temporarily estranged one from the other. She yearns for the restoration of their relationship for, as she says, she is “weak with love” (The Bride 10). Consider the contrast: while he celebrates marital love in its fresh newness, she celebrates on-going, persistent love, yearning for an ever-growing, developing love with her husband.

overview.title: How to read Isaiah.
overview.content:
Have you known religious people who lived double lives? Isaiah confronted the hypocrisy of his own people and challenged them to change their ways, return to God, and love him with all their hearts and minds.

Isaiah contains some of the Old Testament’s most memorable and eloquent writings. This powerful book is as compelling today as it was back then: saturated with passion and power, filled with testimonies of God’s faithfulness to his promises, and a thrilling glimpse of the future as God’s salvation reaches the ends of the earth. Isaiah’s prophecies about Jesus have inspired countless generations to worship our amazing King and Savior.

Isaiah vividly depicts the many facets of God’s character. We see him at work in both judgment and mercy, in times of discipline and of grace, justice and forgiveness, exile and salvation. The multi-faceted involvement of God in human history fills the pages of Isaiah, waiting on your response —will it be of faith or unbelief?

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Isaiah son of Amoz is the author. His prophetic ministry spanned six decades (740–680 bc). The prophetic oracles in his book are organized chronologically and occurred during the reigns of four kings of Judah: Uzziah (1:1-5:30), Jotham (6:1-13), Ahaz (7:1-12:6), and Hezekiah (13:1-66:24). Chapters 36-39 parallel the events of Hezekiah’s reign that are also spoken of in 2Ki 18-20 and 2Ch 29-32. This historical recap in Isaiah underscores the events that framed his prophetic ministry. It speaks of the defining moment at the outset of Hezekiah’s reign: the miraculous deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib’s seige in 722 bc in the wake of the northern kingdom’s fall to Assyria. Two key episodes that marked the end of his reign are also retold in this passage: the miraculous deliverance from his near-death illness and the fateful visit of the Babylonian ambassadors. These events set the stage for the upcoming judgment and exile of Judah. Isaiah’s prophetic role was central to all of these pivotal moments in Judah’s history.

overview.title: How does it fit in the big story?
overview.content: Isaiah lived in the southern kingdom of Judah. During his lifetime, the northern kingdom of Israel fell to the Assyrians. In 722 bc as the northern kingdom was swept from the map, he preached a message of repentance to Judah. Because the people heeded Isaiah and turned to God, Judah was delivered from the Assyrians and was spared the fate of their northern cousins.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Isaiah is the voice behind both the green and the black sections of this book. As you read through the 19 green sections pay special attention to the phrases in which Isaiah’s passions, perspective, and personality show through with great clarity. You can see that he is a man deeply concerned for his people and totally devoted to his God.

Though there are blue sections throughout the book, the bulk of the blue material is found in the historical interlude of chapters 36-39. It is interesting to note that the red sections are three times more frequent after chapter 40 than before it. In chapters 1-39 the red words of God occupy only 20.7 percent of the text; in chapters 40-66 they occupy 61.8 percent.

Isaiah is rich in predictive material. Most of it is found in the red sections as God speaks of the future. As you read through these passages, read with care for God often fast-forwards unexpectedly from events in Isaiah’s day to those coming a few generations after his, and then on to the advent of the Messiah and his redeeming sacrifice, only then to rush forward to the ultimate establishment of God’s kingdom on the earth. If you find it challenging to figure out the timing and sequence of all of the predictive elements in these telescoping prophecies, don’t lose heart. You are not the only one who has struggled to understand when some of these things would come to pass. Even the prophets themselves “wondered what time or situation the Spirit of Christ within them was talking about when he told them in advance about Christ’s suffering and his great glory afterward” (1Pe 1:11). Focus instead on the central message of Isaiah: the story of God’s incredible and merciful salvation not only to those living in Judah then, but also to us living in our day.

overview.title: How to read Jeremiah.
overview.content:
Ever struggled with knowing and doing God’s will? You’re in good company. Jeremiah’s journey of obedience reveals the challenges he experienced as he sought to understand and fulfill God’s purposes for his life. Even after he’d made his choice to obey God, many pressures and persecution made him wonder if he’d done the right thing. In the process, Jeremiah wrestles with some of life’s most difficult questions—questions that we too may confront in life’s journey. Reading Jeremiah gives us an opportunity to gain insight and perspective on what it means to serve God even in difficult times. It helps us discover practical wisdom for everyday life as we seek to follow God in the face of opposition.

Jeremiah shares with us much of his own story, more than most prophets do. His personal transparency helps us experience not only his challenging circumstances, but his feelings about those experiences. His identification with God’s broken heart over his people, and his description of God’s identification with his own suffering, sometimes makes it a challenge to know who is speaking. Are the words describing God’s emotions, Jeremiah’s, or both?

Jeremiah warns Judah about God’s coming judgment with passionate, often tearful speeches. These were hard words to speak, but they were never spoken harshly. They evidence God’s pain as he tirelessly reaches out to a resistant people and is forced to bring judgment upon their persistent sins. That’s why even amidst warnings of impending destruction we discover promises of hope about Judah’s future redemption.

Jeremiah—like many Old Testament prophets—focuses not so much on predicting the future, but on preaching to his contemporaries, seeking to woo them back to a loving relationship with God. The prophets serve as cultural commentators, holding their society accountable to the standards of the covenant (Deu 27-30). They warn the people of the impending judgment in the hope that they might repent and avail themselves of the mercy of God. The prophets understood that the interplay of God’s justice and mercy is designed to draw people back into the covenantal relationship with himself.

Though this thinking underlies the whole of the prophetic tradition, perhaps it is most clearly stated in the encounter between Jeremiah and God at the potter’s shop (Jer 18:1-12). God explains to Jeremiah that when he speaks of judgment, it is not an irrevocable situation. He speaks of judgment always with the hope that people will turn and meet the conditions that allow him to demonstrate mercy. Here is the principle: God will always uphold justice, but he’s always looking for every opportunity to display mercy.

After seeing this principle played out in the history of his own rebellious people, Jeremiah would later reflect on how God’s mercy transcends God’s judgments. He wrote, “The faithful love of the Lord never ends! His mercies never cease. Great is his faithfulness; his mercies begin afresh each morning . . . Though he brings grief, he also shows compassion because of the greatness of his unfailing love. For he does not enjoy hurting people or causing them sorrow” (Lam 3:22-23, 32-33).

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Jeremiah, a priest and prophet. Jeremiah dictated these prophecies to his aide, Baruch.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Over the course of Jeremiah’s ministry (626–580 bc). The last verses were added after 561 bc, about 25 years after the destruction of Jerusalem.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: Jeremiah watched his nation fall apart morally from within and be destroyed militarily from without. He saw idolatry of his fellow citizens which led ultimately to the siege and sack of Jerusalem (586 bc). Even when many people were taken captive, his warnings and pleas that Judah should turn back to God fell on deaf ears.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
In Jeremiah’s rich dramatic writings there are 705 voice changes. There are 29 different individuals and groups that make up the cast of the blue text. All together Jeremiah is 5 percent blue, 10 percent green, 27 percent black and 58 percent red. As you read the red words of God, take note of the many promises he makes—promises of both judgment and of salvation.

Even though God has the biggest part of this book, take time to listen carefully to Jeremiah’s voice reflected not only in the green but also in the black text of this book. Pay attention to all the feelings revealed and consider how you can identify more fully with the heart of God for the lost of the world.

It may help you understand the book if you realize that the prophecies of Jeremiah don’t always follow each other in the order they were given. Instead of being organized chronologically, they are grouped together around various themes. Look back at 2 Kings 23-25 and 2 Chronicles 34-36 to read about the reigns of the last five kings of Judah (Josiah, Jehoahaz, Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin, Zedekiah) and the first Babylonian-appointed governor (Gedaliah). It was during the reigns of these leaders that Jeremiah’s ministry (626–585 bc) took place. See the following chart to understand during whose reign the different prophetic oracles were delivered:
• King Josiah (640–609 bc)
• King Jehoahaz/Shallum (3 months in 609 bc)
• King Jehoiakim/Eliakim (609–Dec 598 bc)
• King Jehoiachin/Jeconiah (Dec 598–Mar 597 bc)
• King Zedekiah (Mar 597–Jul 586 bc)
• Governor Gedaliah (Jul 586 bc forward)

overview.title: How to read Lamentations.
overview.content:
Anger, desperation, fear, loneliness, hopelessness . . . Jerusalem has just fallen to her enemies! In the midst of this devastating chapter of her story, Jeremiah gives voice to Jerusalem’s agony. Stripped of everything but their hope in God, Jeremiah deals with the reality of pain and suffering. In Jerusalem’s despair and disaster, he looks suffering squarely in the face and brings a carefully crafted word of hope based on God’s character.

Each of the five chapters of Lamentations has a slightly different theme, revolving around the middle chapter’s exaltation of God’s compassion and faithfulness. He brings new mercies with each sunrise. Though judgment is deserved, there’s more than justice on display here, for God is a God of constant grace and mercy.

As you read Lamentations, look for contrasting themes of despair and hope, repentance and renewal—for individuals, cities, and nations. Notice also the book’s careful construction. Jeremiah used an alphabetic acrostic to compose these poems expressing the full extent of his pain and sorrow—as though including everything from A to Z. As the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet give start to each line, we are aware that Jeremiah’s passion is expressed with studied care. This heartrending account is the fruit of thoughtful meditation, not only on the tragic circumstances, but also on God’s wonderful character.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: Probably Jeremiah.

overview.title: When was it written and why?
overview.content: Soon after Jerusalem’s fall to Babylon in 586 bc, perhaps within a decade. Jeremiah had travailed with his people for years before this happened, warning and pleading with the nation as he knew disaster was coming if they did not turn. In deep agony, he wrote this poem to express his nation’s grief.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content: The lead character in this book is a city! Jerusalem, recently destroyed by the Babylonians, is personified and expresses herself in the green text of Lamentations. All those in the supporting cast (in blue) are related to Jerusalem. They include her children, her scoffers, her enemies, her people, and her most famous citizen: Jeremiah! You can easily see the dramatic interplay in the following chart which shows how many words are spoken in each part of the text:

overview.title: How to read Ezekiel.
overview.content:
The nation was in crises. Turmoil swirled around them. The changing political realities in Assyria, Egypt, and Babylon threatened Jerusalem’s very existence. As major empires shifted, God sought to call his wayward people back to himself. He “repeatedly sent his prophets to warn them, for he had compassion on his people and his Temple” (2Ch 36:15). These prophets included Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel, whose ministries overlapped with one another. “But the people mocked these messengers of God and despised their words . . . So the Lord brought the king of Babylon against them” (2Ch 36:16-17).

The real story behind the political headlines is that of a God who will go to any lengths to pursue his people and woo them back to himself. Judgment is the last thing God wants to bring upon his people. God would rather forgive than judge, but he will judge if necessary. Given their persistent disobedience God asks, “Do you think that I like to see wicked people die? . . . Of course not! I want them to turn from their wicked ways and live. However, if righ-teous people turn from their righ-teous behavior and start doing sinful things and act like other sinners, should they be allowed to live? No, of course not!” (Eze 18:23-24). Therefore he prophesies impending doom, hoping that the people will repent—and that judgment can be averted. This is the desire of God’s heart. He passionately cries out, “I don’t want you to die . . . Turn back and live!” (Eze 18:32).

Ezekiel is very gifted artistically. He performs plays. He writes poetry. He describes some of the most visually rich visions ever seen. If you can’t picture all the details of some of these amazing prophetic experiences, don’t worry. Ezekiel is describing the indescribable. As he says, “This is what the glory of the Lord looked like to me” (Eze 1:28). His word pictures challenge our imagination and point us to faith in a God who is out of the ordinary—an amazing God of great majesty, power, and glory! God is even capable of making whitened skeletons live again (chapter 37). Allow these extraordinary pictures to resonate in your heart. Let them strengthen your hope in a God who powerfully restores life from the ashes. Let them increase your hunger for renewed intimacy with a God who longs for you. Let them refocus you to live fully for his highest glory.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and why?
overview.content: Ezekiel, a priest taken captive from Jerusalem by the armies of Babylon. It was there—exiled among pagans—that he had his visions of God. Ezekiel wrote to his fellow Jews living in exile between 593 and 571 bc. They needed to know that the God of Israel was still God—even in the hostile environment of idolatrous Babylon.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content:
Three times the armies of Babylon would overwhelm Jerusalem. In the first (605 bc), King Jehoiakim was taken captive to Babylon along with other prominent citizens, including Daniel and his three friends. The second time Jerusalem was overrun by Babylonian forces (597 bc), King Jehoiachin and a notable entourage were sent into exile. Among them was the young priest Ezekiel. Though these two blows to Jerusalem were devastating, nothing would compare to the Babylonian invasion of 586 bc. This time Jerusalem and her famous temple would be totally destroyed and all her citizens deported by force. Ezekiel heard the terrible news “on January 8, during the twelfth year of our captivity” (Narrator 87) when a survivor told him, “The city has fallen!” (A Man from Jerusalem 1).

This is what Ezekiel had feared would happen. Up to this point his prophetic message (chapters 1-32) had been one of warning, urging Judah and other nations to repent in hopes that judgment might be averted. After Jerusalem’s fall, his prophetic message shifted to one of hope (chapters 33-48). He encouraged the Jewish exiles that not all was lost and that—if they turned back to God—there would be a new temple and they would be restored as a people.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
God’s words fill this book. Nearly 82 percent of the text is in red (a little more than 17 percent is in black; while green and blue together make up less than 1 percent). Some of these red passages are spoken by “a man in linen” (God 36) or “a man whose face shone like bronze” (God 92-98,103-106). These are prophetic images that Ezekiel has of Christ speaking.

God asks more than fifty questions in this book (can you find them all?). Some, directed personally to Ezekiel, create the framework of a unique visual dialogue between God and this visionary prophet. God asks him:

1. Son of man . . . do you see . . . ? (God 19-20)

2. Do you see . . . ? (God 20)

3. Son of man, have you seen . . . ? (God 24)

4. Have you seen this? (God 26)

5. Have you seen this, son of man? (God 28)

6. Have you been watching, son of man? (God 105)

Ezekiel’s perspective is reflected in both the black and the green words of this book. There’s a marked difference in the role of the Narrator in the first part of the book compared to that in the last part. About half of the black words occur in the first 98 times the Narrator speaks (chapters 1-39). The other half occur in just the last 16 times the Narrator speaks (chapters 40-48). Also of note is the longest green passage (Ezekiel 5). It is spoken out by Ezekiel just after his wife dies, a woman who is called Ezekiel’s “dearest treasure” (God 66). His personal tragedy becomes an object lesson for the whole nation. So much love, sorrow, and unfulfilled hopes are mingled together in this poignant image of controlled mourning. Ponder on this prophecy and allow God to give you a fresh insight into his heart.

Finally, if you are still struggling with understanding the exotic images and the challenging message of Ezekiel, be encouraged as you read the blue passages of the book. There are only two of them. One announces the fall of Jerusalem (A Man from Jerusalem 1). The other reflects the reaction of Ezekiel’s audience. They said, “What does all this mean? What are you trying to tell us?” (The People 1). So, you are not alone. Take heart. Keep asking questions, keep reading, and keep listening to God!

overview.title: How to read Daniel.
overview.content:
Ever wished God would show himself so powerfully that it would strike awe into the hearts of doubters? We all have, and in Daniel this is exactly what happens. God’s miraculous power and amazing majesty are seen through unbelievable rescues and extraordinary predictions. On display is God’s rulership over the affairs of mankind both present and future: he is indeed the king over all kings and his kingdom will have no end!

The book of Daniel divides into two sections. In the first (chapters 1-6), Daniel serves as an interpreter of dreams and becomes a trusted royal counselor. Because of his unblemished integrity Daniel advised kings and influenced the top rulers of both the Babylonian and Medo-Persian empires over a seventy-year time span. Daniel and his friends made a powerful impact on the surrounding pagan society as they loved and trusted God in a hostile environment.

In the second section (chapters 7-12), Daniel is a dreamer of dreams. The visions he has detail some of the most awesome predictions in Scripture. God shows him the great sweep of history, highlighting the rise and fall of the Babylonian, Medo-Persian, Greek and Roman kingdoms. Most every prophecy culminates with the kingdom of God breaking into the kingdom of men with the coming of the Messiah. This unique view of history from the perspective of God’s throne room filled the people then—and us now—with great hope, anticipating the eternal reign of the King of Kings.

The following historical synopsis of the five major predictive passages of Daniel may help you grasp the powerful message of this extraordinary book:

overview.title: Who wrote it and when?
overview.content: Daniel—whose name means “God is [my] judge.” Possibly a member of Judah’s royal family, he was taken captive to Babylon around 605 bc, at the time of Babylon’s first invasion of Jerusalem. The incredible detail of future events leads some to contend it must have been written after many of the predicted events had occurred. But, those who believe God can reveal future events place it between 536 and 530 bc—soon after Cyrus of Persia conquered Babylon in 539 bc.

overview.title: How does it fit into God’s big story?
overview.content: The Babylonians (626–539 bc) had replaced Assyria as the world’s superpower. Nebuchadnezzar’s army conquered Judah, taking thousands to Babylon. Jerusalem and the temple were totally destroyed. While Daniel was exiled in Babylon, he reminded the people of Judah of God’s ultimate control over history. His message was that God’s kingdom would ultimately triumph over all human authority. Through the coming Messiah “the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that will never be destroyed or conquered . . . it will stand forever” (Dan 2:44).

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Of all the prophets, Daniel has the most blue text. It totals 31 percent of the book. This is followed by the black (28 percent), green (23 percent) and red (18 percent) sections. Thirteen individuals and groups comprise the supporting cast of Daniel. Ten of them are pagan kings and members of their royal entourage:
• King Nebuchadnezzar together with A Herald, Arioch, Ashpenaz, The Astrologers and The King’s Advisers;
• King Belshazzar with The Queen Mother; and
• Darius the Mede with Darius’s Officials.
• An Angel and Gabriel.
• Daniel’s Three Friends.

Though these Hebrew boys speak only once in the book, their words powerfully capture the central message of the book: how to be a faithful representative of the kingdom of God when living among the godless and idolatrous kingdoms of men.

They are seeking to be faithful to God in the midst of a pagan society. The king has demanded that all worship a gold statue on pain of death. They refuse. When they are dragged before the king, their defense is a magnificent example for all future generations to follow. Their faith leads them to have an incredible confidence in God’s ability to save, but an unswerving commitment to worship him alone no matter what circumstances may befall them. They boldly declare, “If we are thrown into the blazing furnace, the God whom we serve is able to save us. He will rescue us from your power, Your Majesty. But even if he doesn’t, we want to make it clear to you, Your Majesty, that we will never serve your gods or worship the gold statue you have set up.”

That phrase—“But even if he doesn’t”—reflects the attitude of those who serve God with their whole heart—an attitude that makes them more than conquerors—no matter what happens! As you read the amazing stories contained in this book, may you too be encouraged to live such a whole-hearted, undivided, and radical devotion to God!

overview.title: How to read Hosea.
overview.content:
Adultery! This kind of scandal is the stuff of today’s talk show! The unfaithfulness of an adulterous wife would devastate any husband. But in Hosea’s case the private pain was compounded by public humiliation because his wife was a prostitute. This graphic, heart-wrenching story of the prophet Hosea and his wife Gomer illustrates another love story gone awry. The gripping story of one man’s pain can only woo our hearts back to the Lover of our souls. Hosea’s agony over and faithfulness towards his wayward wife depicts God’s constant love for his disobedient people.

The first three chapters of Hosea describe his family life, especially the adultery of his wife, which exemplifies Israel’s unfaithfulness to God. The rest of the book contains vivid messages on the sins of the people and God’s passionate desire for them to return to him. Throughout this book you’ll experience in dramatic fashion a portrait of a brokenhearted God who will execute justice if necessary, but who prefers to show mercy if possible, because he loves his people so dearly.

Notice the stark consequences of sin as God states his case against his people. But then look beyond Hosea’s suffering (and God’s pain) to see an example of love that will not quit. Look for ways that Hosea loved his undeserving wife and consider how God has done the same for you.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Hosea, a prophet to the northern kingdom of Israel, began prophesying around 750 bc and continued until the northern kingdom fell to Assyrian forces in 722 bc. During Hosea’s ministry six ungodly kings ruled on the throne of Israel. The final demise of Israel began with Jeroboam II, a wicked king whose leadership had produced a materialistic, immoral, and unjust society. Those who followed him on the throne only accelerated Israel’s collapse. This book was probably compiled shortly after Israel’s destruction in 722 bc.

overview.title: How does it fit in God’s big story?
overview.content: After Assyria had conquered Israel, Hosea’s words were written down as a record of a prophecy that had been fulfilled. It also served as a warning—possibly to the remnant left behind in Israel, or to the people of the southern kingdom of Judah, who were facing their own spiritual and social challenges. Would Judah go the way of Israel or would they turn back to God?

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Hosea is the first of the “minor” prophets—minor, not in the importance of their message, but in the length of their written work (as compared to Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel). The following chart gives an overview, showing the percentage of text color found in each of the minor prophets: The Narrator (always black); God (always red); the book’s lead character (green); and the Supporting Cast (blue). These last two necessarily change from book to book:
• Hosea
• Joel
• Amos
• Obadiah
• Jonah
• Micah
• Nahum
• Habakkuk
• Zephaniah
• Haggai
• Zechariah
• Malachi

In Hosea there are only two blue speakers. Of the two, the Israelites have the larger part: they speak four times. The first three times we read what they actually said. Note the disdain for the prophets (The Israelites 1), the scorn for political leaders (The Israelites 2), and the prideful boasting in their self-made wealth (The Israelites 3). The fourth time is what Hosea hoped they would say. But—alas!—these words were never spoken with repentant hearts.

There are only two green passages in this book—passages in which Hosea’s heart is revealed. In the first he calls his wife back to himself; in the second he calls Israel back to God. The passion of these words still speaks powerfully to us today: “Come, let us return to the Lord. He has torn us to pieces; now he will heal us. He has injured us; now he will bandage our wounds. In just a short time he will restore us, so that we may live in his presence. Oh, that we might know the Lord! Let us press on to know him. He will respond to us as surely as the arrival of dawn or the coming of rains in early spring” (Hos 6:1-3).

overview.title: How to read Joel.
overview.content:
In the midst of a dark moment in Israel’s story, Joel gives voice to God’s intense desire for intimacy with all his people. The wickedness of idolatry surely warrants judgment. But Joel goes beyond warnings of doom to passionate promises of restoration, which would culminate in an outpouring of God’s own Spirit on all people. Women and men, young and old alike (2:28-29) would all have intimate fellowship with God and minister in his power!

Be alert to God’s call on your life as you read through the pages of this book. Joel reminds you that God wants your heart. He wants your love. He wants your life. He is serious about your walk with him. There is no room for sin, but do not fear. The message—both then and now—is clear: “Return to the Lord your God, for he is merciful and compassionate, slow to get angry and filled with unfailing love” (2:13).

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Joel, a prophet of God. Some think he wrote after the Jews returned from exile in Babylon (538 bc). But because Joel mentions no king and speaks of elders as leaders (1:2), others suggest a much earlier date—perhaps around 835 bc when Joash of Judah was still a child (2Ki 11:21).

overview.title: How does it fit in God’s big story?
overview.content: Seven-year-old Joash had just been crowned king over Judah. Under Joash’s wicked father and grandmother, pagan idol worship had flourished (2Ki 8:25–11:21). But now that they had died, Joel urged the people of Judah to turn back to God. With young Joash, Joel saw great opportunity for renewal in the land. But he also warned Judah that judgment—an agricultural disaster of epic proportions—would come if they did not repent.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Joel is one of only three minor prophets that has no green text (see the introduction to Hosea). Yet we hear the intensity of Joel’s exhortation to Judah through the black words of the Narrator. Pay attention to his abundant use of imperatives to wake his audience to action:
• The Narrator 1—Hear . . . listen . . . tell your children . . . pass the story down . . . wake up . . . weep . . . wail . . . weep . . . despair . . . wail . . . weep . . . dress yourselves in burlap . . . weep . . . wail . . . come . . . spend the night in burlap . . . announce . . . call . . . bring . . . cry out . . . sound the alarm . . . raise the battle cry . . . tremble in fear.
• The Narrator 2—Return . . . blow the ram’s horn . . . announce . . . call . . . gather . . . call . . . stand . . . weep . . . pray.
• The Narrator 4—Don’t be afraid . . . be glad now . . . rejoice . . . don’t be afraid . . . rejoice . . . rejoice.
• God 1—Turn to me . . . give me your hearts . . . come . . . don’t tear your clothing . . . tear your hearts.
• God 5—Get ready . . . call out . . . advance . . . hammer . . . train . . . come . . . gather.
• God 6—Be called to arms . . . march . . . swing the sickle . . . come . . .tread the grapes.

Finally, we note that there is but one blue passage—a desperate prayer in which the priests cry out to God for mercy. Their words have the same imploring intensity as they address heaven: “Spare your people, Lord!” (Joe 2:17).

overview.title: How to read Amos.
overview.content:
Ever feel helpless in the face of overwhelming injustice? We often feel so unqualified. After all, who would listen if we spoke up? Can we really make a difference when injustice is widespread? This is the story of a man who was as unqualified as any of us could ever be. Amos was a simple shepherd, but he took on the power structures of his day and brought God’s perspective to that complacent society. So, let Amos’s story stir your heart to action and faith! If God could use him to impact his generation, certainly he could use you to shape yours.

Amos and Hosea stand out as the two prophetic books directed primarily to the northern kingdom of Israel. While Hosea focuses on Israel’s idolatry, Amos takes a hard look at the rampant injustice in Israel’s society. These two books complement each other wonderfully for they address the two sins most often mentioned in the prophetic tradition. The prophets held people accountable when they failed to love God with all their heart (idolatry) or when they did not love their neighbors as themselves (injustice).

With blunt poetic imagery, Amos speaks passionately about how God feels when the wealthy and the powerful exploit the poor and the defenseless. Although these words were directed to the ungodly power brokers of Israel, their message still reverberates with contemporary immediacy. Watch for parallels between Amos’s time and ours. When he speaks of the poor, think of the homeless, ethnic minorities, single parents, the elderly, and others who live on the margins of our society. What would Amos call us to do?

Amos’s book divides into three main sections. The first two chapters contain a series of messages against six nearby nations (Da-mas-cus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, and Moab) and against the southern kingdom of Judah and the northern kingdom of Israel. The next four chapters record messages of judgment from God against “the entire family [God] rescued from Egypt” (The Narrator 11). The last three chapters record five visions of destruction foretelling Israel’s fall to Assyria in 722 bc.
• Vision 1: The Narrator 38–40a
• Vision 2: The Narrator 40b–42a
• Vision 3: The Narrator 42b–Amos 7
• Vision 4: The Narrator 48–God 39
• Vision 5: The Narrator 54–59

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Amos wrote it toward the end of Jeroboam II’s reign (786–746 bc), during a time of economic growth and prosperity in the northern kingdom. He was a shepherd from Judah with no known ministry credentials—except a word from God.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: This message was directed to the people of Israel, the northern kingdom. Amos, from the southern kingdom of Judah, challenged their materialistic greed and pleasure-seeking lifestyle which resulted in great social inequities. In a time when many were exploiting the poor, Amos wrote to remind them that God takes special care of the disadvantaged. His prophecy was God’s last appeal to Israel, warning them to repent before it was too late.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: The leadership of Jeroboam II had enabled Israel to flourish. Politically and militarily, his reign constituted Israel’s golden years. He won battles and expanded the kingdom’s territory (2Ki 14:23-29). Israel came to know great wealth and power. On the surface nothing could be better, but the moral fiber of the nation was disintegrating. And a great danger was lurking in the shadows. In only a little more than two decades after Jeroboam II’s death, this seemingly powerful nation would be completely wiped off the face of the map by rival Assyria!

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Though Amos’s perspective is reflected in the (black) words of the Narrator we only hear his voice directly in eight (green) passages. Read through these passages and you discover some interesting repetitions.

First, God shows him two visions which portray coming judgment. In both instances, Amos immediately intercedes for Israel. He asks God to suspend the judgment because “Israel is so small” (Amos 4 & 5). Of course this is not how Israel saw itself, for they were reveling in the heyday of their national expansion. Amos understood that God was not as impressed with Israel as they were with themselves. In both instances, the Narrator tells us that God heard Amos’s prayers “and relented from this plan” (Narrator 39 & 41).

But these two visions are followed by two more and in these Israel does not fare so well. Amos’s two shortest statements (Amos 6 & 8) are made in response to God’s repeated question, “Amos, what do you see?” (God 33 & 35). First Amos saw a plumb line. Then he saw a basket full of ripe fruit. Both were images of judgment. But, unlike the earlier visions, God goes on to tell him that judgment will not be averted. Israel had not turned from its sin; therefore its fate was sealed.

overview.title: How to read Obadiah.
overview.content:
Has anyone ever taken advantage of you—and gotten away with it? Perhaps friends or even family members have turned on you when you needed them to stand by you. Obadiah tells the compelling story of how God loves his people and is unwaveringly loyal to them in just such a time. This is evident by the judgment he pronounced against the Edomites who had unjustly taken advantage of his people.

Don’t miss this tiny book, tucked in between the better-known books of Amos and Jonah. You’ll miss vital truths. Obadiah addresses the issue of pride and the desire for vengeance. This book shows you that these attitudes ultimately lead to destruction—as they did for the people of Edom. The Edomites relied on their mountain stronghold and their military might, and they learned a painful lesson: Whenever you find security in your own strength, you’re on shaky ground. True strength and security come from God alone.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content:
Obadiah, an otherwise unknown prophet. His name means the “servant of the Lord.” Because Edom was unfriendly towards the people of Judah several times during their history, it’s not certain which of these events the book addresses. Therefore, he may have written it:
• between 849 and 841 bc (during Jehoram’s reign; see 2Ki 8:16-24; 2Ch 21:8-10,16-17), or
• between 732 and 716 bc (during Ahaz’ reign; see 2Ch 28:16-19),but it’s more likely he wrote it
• between 605 and 586 bc (at the time of Jerusalem’s fall to Babylon; see Psa 137:1-9).

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content:
Edomites were the descendants of Esau, Jacob/Israel’s half-brother. For generations there had been a long-standing feud between the people of Judah and the neighboring nation of Edom, their “close relatives” (God 3). It seems that when the nation of Judah “was invaded” (God 3) by Babylon, the Edomites not only cheered Jerusalem’s destruction, they looted Judah in the aftermath. They even captured those who tried to escape and turned them over to the Babylonian enemy. Obadiah condemns Edom’s treachery and declares God’s coming justice for their war crimes against his people.

Some think that the reference to the Edomite “rock fortress . . . high in the mountains” (God 1) is an allusion to the area where the ruins of Petra in modern-day Jordan stand.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Obadiah is only one of two minor prophets that has no blue text (the other is Habakkuk). With only one short green passage, this book has the highest percentage of red text of any of the minor prophets (see the chart in the introduction to Hosea). God obviously has a lot to say about treachery!

As you read through this short book consider:
• What did the Edomites do?
• What did others do?
• What does God say he will do?

overview.title: How to read Jonah.
overview.content:
If you’ve ever thought that some people are beyond hope—so evil that they are incapable of change—the book of Jonah may upset your thinking. Consider serial killers, rapists, drug kingpins, or terrorists: It’s not unnatural to wish that such violent, hate-filled individuals would be punished. But this book shows us that God wants to extend his grace and mercy to even the worst of people. It also challenges us to see that God may want to use us to reach out to the very people we may despise or see as beyond redemption.

Masterfully written, this book seeks to grab your attention from the opening sentence to the question at the end. Read looking for the implications of the call of God on Jonah’s life—and on yours. God’s boundless compassion shattered Jonah’s bitter prejudice against Nineveh, the powerful capital of Assyria, which had sorely oppressed Israel. God’s eagerness to forgive the repentant sinner exposed the narrowness of Jonah’s heart and illustrates for us the greatness of God’s love. When Jonah grumbled against God for showing grace to Israel’s enemies, God’s compassionate response was “ Shouldn’t I feel sorry for such a great city?” (4:11).

As you read, look for passages that show God’s compassion for all people. Note his desire for repentance and forgiveness regardless of what someone has done, and the extraordinary lengths to which he will go to get our attention. Rejoice as you observe the book’s central truth that God wants the Good News of salvation to be proclaimed to all people who do not know him.

overview.title: Who wrote it?
overview.content: Most likely the prophet Jonah wrote autobiographically about what had happened to him.

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: Probably between 785 and 750 BC, during the reign of Jeroboam II, the king of Israel (see 2Ki 14:25).

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To tell the story of God’s concern even for the enemies of his people and to show how God used a reluctant prophet as a vehicle of his grace.

overview.title: What was happening at that time?
overview.content: Israel’s northern kingdom had regained its influence under Jeroboam II. But the Assyrians, whose capital city was Nineveh, were asserting themselves in increasingly menacing ways.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Of all the minor prophets Jonah’s cast presents the most evenly spread distribution of color in the SourceView text. The Narrator’s words occupy 51 percent of the text, God’s 9 percent, Jonah’s 27 percent, and everyone else’s (The Ship’s Captain, The Ship’s Crew, and Nineveh’s King & Nobles) 13 percent.

One of the key things to observe are the many questions raised in this book. Not one of the speaking roles is without a question. God asks three questions:
• “Is it right for you to be angry about this?” (God 3)
• “Is it right for you to be angry because the plant died?” (God 4)
• “Shouldn’t I feel sorry for such a great city?” (God 5)

Jonah asks one revealing question:
• “Didn’t I say before I left home that you would do this, Lord?” (Jonah 6).

The captain asks one question:
• “How can you sleep at a time like this?” (The Ship’s Captain 1).

The inquisitive crew ask a total of seven questions:
• “Why has this awful storm come down on us?” (The Ship’s Crew 1)
• “Who are you?” (The Ship’s Crew 2)
• “What is your line of work?” (The Ship’s Crew 2)
• “What country are you from?” (The Ship’s Crew 2)
• “What is your nationality?” (The Ship’s Crew 2)
• “Oh, why did you do it?” (The Ship’s Crew 3)
• “What should we do to you to stop this storm?” (The Ship’s Crew 4)
• “Who can tell?” (Nineveh’s King & Nobles 1).

Another element that stands out with the SourceView format is we see a huge emphasis on prayer. The longest participation of each of the human actors has them either in prayer or addressing the issue of prayer:
• 59 percent of Jonah’s words in this book are his prayer from inside of the fish (Jonah 4)
• The captain’s primary command is to pray (The Ship’s Captain 2)
• The crew pray desperately to God for their lives depend on it (The Ship’s Crew 5-6); and
• The leadership of Nineveh issue a decree to all its citizens to fast and “everyone must pray earnestly to God” (Nineveh’s King & Nobles 1).

In light of this, consider what new measures of prayer would God call you to in order to see your world transformed?

overview.title: How to read Micah.
overview.content:
Micah’s predictive clarity, coupled with his vivid insight into the ways of God, makes this book a very compelling read. Standing on the threshold of some pivotal events in Israel’s history, Micah looks into the future and prophesies landmark moments with pinpoint accuracy, including:
• the fall of Israel in 722 bc,
• the siege (and deliverance) of Jerusalem in 701 bc,
• the fall of Jerusalem in 605/597/586 bc,
• the initial restoration of the exiles back to Jerusalem in 536/516 bc, and
• the ultimate restoration of God’s purposes through the coming of Messiah.

In four prophetic sequences, Micah begins with his day and telescopes through these future events until he reaches the New Testament era.
• 1:1-2:11 Judgment and destruction
• 2:12-13 Mercy and restoration
• 3:1-12 Judgment and destruction
• 4:1-8 Mercy and restoration
• 4:9-5:1 Judgment and destruction
• 5:2-15 Mercy and restoration
• 6:1-7:6 Judgment and destruction
• 7:7-20 Mercy and restoration

Through passionate poetry and vivid figures of speech, Micah creates a stunning portrait of God’s character. The message alternates between words of judgment and words of hope, between God enacting justice and displaying mercy. Micah warned his generation that God’s judgment was approaching because they had rejected God and his law. But he also encouraged the godly that judgment would not be the final word. His ultimate confidence for a hopeful future lies in the character of God. Micah concludes with a signature question: “Where is another God like you, who pardons the guilt of the remnant, overlooking the sins of his special people? You will not stay angry with your people forever, because you delight in showing unfailing love” (Mic 7:18).

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content: The prophet Micah. His name means “Who is like the Lord?” How appropriate! For through his ministry we have a brilliant image of the incomparable character of God—no one else is like the Lord!

overview.title: To whom was it written and when?
overview.content:
According to the first verse of this book, these prophecies came to Micah during the reigns of Jotham (750–735 bc), Ahaz (735–715 bc), and Hezekiah (715–686 bc), kings of Judah. This makes him a contemporary with Isaiah. We also learn from this first verse that though he was from Judah his ministry was to “both Samaria and Jerusalem”—capital cities of the northern and southern kingdoms respectively.

During the years of his ministry he would see Israel destroyed by Assyria (722 bc) and Judah very nearly so. As the Narrator in the book that bears his name, he writes, “My people’s wound is too deep to heal. It has reached into Judah, even to the gates of Jerusalem” (Mic 1:9). This refers to the events of 701 bc when the forces of Sennacherib, king of Assyria, had pushed south and taken most of Judah and had laid seige to Jerusalem (see parallel passages in 2Ki 18:13-19:37; 2Ch 32:1-22; Isa 36:1-37:38).

This was a critical moment in Jewish history. The people found themselves at a crossroads which would determine their future destiny in no uncertain terms. In this moment of great military danger and political upheaval, Micah’s message presented his hearers with two options: continue to disobey God and experience his judgment, or return to God and receive his mercy.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: The powerful Assyrian empire was expanding westward and southward, demanding surrender and tribute. When the northern kingdom of Israel rebelled, the Assyrians destroyed its capital city of Samaria and dispersed its citizens. Israel ceased to exist as a kingdom. Later, Sennacherib deployed his forces southward, taking many in Judah captive. It looked like Jerusalem would go the way of Samaria, but in 701 bc, King Hezekiah of Judah heeded the prophetic words of Micah and Isaiah. He led his people in turning to God in prayer and as a result experienced one of the epic moments of divine deliverance in the history of the Jewish people.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
In times of momentous distress crucial questions are raised. In this book we find questions on display in all four colors. Can you find all 22?
• Micah’s enemies ask one (blue) question designed to undermine his confidence in God.
• God asks six (red) questions of the inhabitants of Jerusalem (three in God 11 and three in God 12) revealing his heart and exposing their sin.
• Micah asks the remaining 15 questions; 14 in his capacity as The Narrator (in black) and one as himself (in green). The greatest bulk of these are bundled together in Narrator 22. Here, Micah fires off five back-to-back questions:
• What can we bring to the Lord?
• What kind of offerings should we give him?
• Should we bow before God with offerings of yearling calves?
• Should we offer him thousands of rams and ten thousand rivers of olive oil?
• Should we sacrifice our firstborn children to pay for our sins?

overview.title: How to read Nahum.
overview.content:
Injustice, cruelty, exploitation . . . at times deep anger is a righ-teous response. Nahum assures us that our anger over sin is matched by God’s. The judge of all the earth does not stand by indifferently. He will not allow evil to persist forever.

This book is the sequel to Jonah. In the former book, Jonah preached destruction but—when the city and its leaders repented—God extended mercy to them. Now we’re back in Nineveh. Years have gone by and the inhabitants of this pagan city have resumed their wickedness. This time there is no repentance and God brings devastating judgment against this powerful Assyrian city.

Nahum displays God as infinitely holy and just (1:2), awesomely all-powerful (1:3), full of goodness and mercy (1:7), and true to his word (1:14). This is just a beginning! Consider what else you can learn about the character of God through the words of the prophet Nahum.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Nahum, an otherwise unknown prophet, whose name means “comfort,” wrote it about a hundred years after Jonah had delivered God’s message to Nineveh, the capital of Assyria. Assyria was at its full strength, arrogant after having conquered Egypt (663 bc) and before it was defeated by Babylon (609 bc).

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: Although the book seems to be addressed to the Assyrians, Nahum’s message is actually for God’s people, the nation of Judah. It assures them that evil does not endure forever and that God will one day fulfill his plan to restore good permanently.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: In 722 bc Assyria defeated the northern kingdom of Israel. Now, almost a hundred years later, the southern kingdom of Judah was ruled by Ma-nas-she, a puppet king controlled by the Assyrians.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
Nahum has no green text and only two words make up the full allotment of blue text spoken by an unknown Someone. As far as the black text goes, The Narrator here has a larger role than in any other book of the minor prophets, as it occupies 85 percent of the book (see the introduction to Hosea).

Even though God’s words (the red text) are few, it’s important to read them carefully. The first word from God is directed toward his people in the kingdom of Judah. Note the promise of deliverance he gives to them. The remaining five words of God are directed to the Assyrians of Nineveh. Note the promises of destruction he gives to them. Because of the use of SourceView color it is easy to see that twice God says to Nineveh, “I am your enemy!” (God 3 & 5). We know from reading Jonah that originally this was not God’s attitude towards them. But their persistence in sin and lack of repentance had changed his course of action towards them. Now their situation was not something to be envied. What a frightful thing it is to have God against you! How much better it would have been for them if they had repented.

overview.title: How to read Habakkuk.
overview.content:
Got hard questions for God? Habakkuk did. And he was not silent about them. The result is a prophetic book like none other. Habakkuk never once delivers a message to the people on God’s behalf. All of his words are directed to God and all of God’s responses are for Habakkuk alone. This produces an engaging and enlightening dialogue as the prophet grapples with God directly over some very hard issues of injustice and evil. If you seek to understand truth and have a passion for justice, Habakkuk’s honest approach is just the thing for you!

Since Habakkuk speaks three times, the book can simply be divided into three sections: the first two record Habakkuk’s hard-hitting questions and the last one is his inspirational prayer of praise. Habakkuk’s first question challenges God for letting the evil of his own people go unpunished. Basically, he says, “God where are you when injustice runs rampant? Why don’t you do something about it?” God responds to his cry, saying, “I will do something about it!” He promises to bring the conquering Babylonians against Jerusalem to execute justice upon his sinful people.

This leads to a second question because God’s first response only seems to make a bad situation worse. Habakkuk cries out in anguished complaint because the Babylonians are even more evil than the evildoers they are coming to destroy. In his response God assures Habakkuk that in due time the Babylonians will also be punished for their wickedness.

As Habakkuk listens to God’s responses, he realizes that God is indeed engaged in the affairs of humanity and will always act with wisdom and equity. This changes his whole outlook on life. He began this book despondent because of the circumstances surrounding him. Now he ends with confidence that justice will ultimately prevail. This new-found hope leads Habakkuk to worship God. But this is no easy, religious cover-up to his earlier concerns. Pay close attention. The finale of the song of praise reveals the transformation that has gone on in his understanding. No longer do the troubling circumstances shape his perspective of God; now the trustworthy character of God shapes the way he looks at circumstances: “Even though the fig trees have no blossoms, and there are no grapes on the vines; even though the olive crop fails, and the fields lie empty and barren; even though the flocks die in the fields, and the cattle barns are empty, yet I will rejoice in the Lord! I will be joyful in the God of my salvation!” (3:17-18).

As you read this poignant little book, consider how you too might look beyond the circumstances of life to the character of God. As you come to know more and more intimately the God who will establish his perfect justice on earth, you too will find reasons for worshipful trust in him—no matter the situation.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Habakkuk, a prophet in the nation of Judah, wrote it at the height of Judah’s waywardness and just before the Babylonians conquered Jerusalem—probably around 610–605 bc.

overview.title: What was happening at this time?
overview.content: Sin was rampant in Judah. The people worshiped idols, sacrificed their children to pagan gods and ignored the one true God. The wicked King Jehoiakim not only refused to listen to God’s prophets, but he burned their writings, arrested several of them, and even murdered one. Jehoiakim foolishly positioned Judah between two warring superpowers—the declining Assyrian empire and the rising Babylonian empire. The historical background to Habakkuk is found in 2Ki 23:31–24:7 and 2Ch 36:1-8.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The text of this book is almost equally divided between Habakkuk’s words (green) and God’s words (red). This visually highlights the nature of the discussion between the prophet and his God. It also makes apparent how seriously God takes the questions of those who pursue truth and understanding. God is not afraid of our hard, honest questions. He’s eager to engage in dialogue with us. As in the case of Job before him (who also grappled with the problem of evil), God’s interaction with the inquirer proves to be transformative.

Consider carefully the sequence of the 13 questions that Habakkuk puts before God. Note that even in his final psalm of praise, Habakkuk incorporates questions into his song. For him worship is not a mindless exercise, but one in which God is intentionally loved with all the strength of our heart, soul and mind!
• Habakkuk 1—How long, O Lord, must I call for help? . . . Must I forever see these evil deeds? . . . Why must I watch all this misery?
• Habakkuk 2—O Lord my God, my Holy One, you who are eternal—surely you do not plan to wipe us out? . . . Will you wink at their treachery? . . . Should you be silent while the wicked swallow up people more righ-teous than they? . . . Are we only fish to be caught and killed? . . . Are we only sea creatures that have no leader? . . . Must we be strung up on their hooks and caught in their nets while they rejoice and celebrate? . . . Will you let them get away with this forever? . . . Will they succeed forever in their heartless conquests?
• Habakkuk 3—Was it in anger, Lord, that you struck the rivers and parted the sea? . . . Were you displeased with them?

But Habakkuk is not the only one that poses questions. God does so as well. Note how his questions link injustice and idolatry—the two most-frequently mentioned sins in the Old Testament. Is this true in your life? Have misplaced passions and wrong priorities (idolatry) led you to wrong actions and unloving responses that have hurt others (injustice)? How so?
• God 2—You’ve become rich by extortion, but how much longer can this go on? . . . Has not the Lord of Heaven’s Armies promised that the wealth of nations will turn to ashes? . . . What good is an idol carved by man, or a cast image that deceives you? . . . Can an idol tell you what to do?

overview.title: How to read Zephaniah.
overview.content:
When justice is distorted, when the line between right and wrong is blurred, when leaders become corrupt, it’s easy to become discouraged. And when religious leaders fall, discouragement can turn to cynicism. Zephaniah reassures us that even when those who are supposed to be examples of good let us down and do wrong, God remains trustworthy. He says: “Its prophets are arrogant liars seeking their own gain. Its priests defile the temple by disobeying God’s instructions. But the Lord is still there in the city, and he does no wrong” (Narrator 12).

Zephaniah is very blunt, painting a bleak picture of gloom and doom. He uses poetry and emotionally charged language to illustrate both God’s judgment and salvation, his correction and compassion. This opportune word gives us marching orders in dark times: we are to burn brightly, to carry the light of the Lord into the darkness, to bring a word of hope for those who turn to the Lord for forgiveness and healing. We can rest assured that God is at work to make everything right in the end. Therefore, Zephaniah ends on a note of triumph and hope, as God’s loving and righ-teous character is revealed.

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Zephaniah, whose name means “the Lord hides” or “the Lord protects,” wrote it during the reign of King Josiah of Judah (640–609 bc, see 1:1), but before the city of Nineveh was destroyed (612 bc, see 2:13).

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: Zephaniah wrote to the people of Judah, warning them of impending judgment for their sins and hoping to stir them to repentance. But he also assured them that God’s judgment would pave the way for a new society in which justice would prevail and all humankind would worship the Lord.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: Despite King Josiah’s well-intentioned civil and religious reforms, many leaders were corrupt and idolatry was still widespread. The Assyrian empire that had pressured Judah for more than a century was disintegrating. Shortly after Josiah died and Zephaniah’s ministry concluded, the Babylonians conquered Judah, destroyed the temple and took many into exile.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
The SourceView colors help point out some key features of this book. First of all, the only blue text highlight the words of proud Nineveh at the zenith of its glory. This helps put the book in its context. It is the middle chapter in a three part prophetic story about Nineveh. The first chapter is written by Jonah, the last one by Nahum. It is helpful to read these three books together as a historical trilogy illustrating the progression of God’s dealings with his people and Assyria.

The longest red section (God 6) and the longest black section (Narrator 7) are found back-to-back. Both have to do with the coming day of the Lord in which his enemies will be judged because “the Lord their God will visit his people in kindness and restore their prosperity again” (Zep 2:7).

In this book it is important to consider the imperatives given to the reader. Note the contrasts as the message develops. The Narrator’s first command is, “Stand in silence”; his almost last one is, “Shout aloud”! Similarly, God’s only imperative spoken towards the beginning is, “Wail in sorrow.” The counterpoint is Zephaniah’s exhortation, “Cheer up” near the conclusion of the book. Reflect on these fifteen commands. What would God want to say to you through them?

These final imperatives take us to Zephaniah’s one and only quotation in the book. After giving these exhortations, he explains why the people should be unafraid and cheerful. He says, “For the Lord your God is living among you. He is a mighty savior. He will take delight in you with gladness. With his love, he will calm all your fears. He will rejoice over you with joyful songs.” Imagine that! The prophet says that this great and mighty God is singing and that you are the subject of his song! If you listen carefully, the lyrics will calm your fears as you hear him tell you of his presence with you, his salvation over you, his delight in you, and his love for you.

overview.title: How to read Haggai.
overview.content:
Come on! Snap out of it! Get going! Ever said that to yourself—or to a loved one—in an attempt to break out of a hopeless and energy-sapping slump? This book is like that. It is a splash of cold water in the face, hitting the blues head on with an invigorating call to action.

Though the Israelites had seen God’s blessing upon them as they returned from exile in Babylon, the people became discouraged when they saw the amount of work needed to rebuild Jerusalem after decades of desolation. It seemed like an overwhelming task. But the people were “greatly encouraged by the preaching of the prophets Haggai and Zechariah” (Ezr 6:14) who ministered together to spur the people of Jerusalem to complete the task of rebuilding the temple. Their message shook their audience out of complacency and got them back on track, provoking them to re-embrace God’s priorities with whole-hearted commitment.

Why did things go wrong with the Israelites? Because they were focusing on building their own houses instead of focusing on building God’s house (Hag 1:9). That’s why Haggai urged them to consider God’s challenge, “Look at what’s happening to you!” (Hag 1:5). This is as important to do now as it was back then. Take time to evaluate the priorities in your life and reflect on what changes you should make in your life’s goals. Are you aligning your priorities with God’s? Is God’s kingdom the top priority in your life or are selfish goals crowding out God’s destiny for your life? What is he saying to you through this book?

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: The prophet Haggai, who delivered his messages in 520 bc. Unlike Jeremiah and Daniel, whose prophetic ministries spanned many decades of life, Haggai’s ministry took place in a very brief time frame. He prophesied on August 29 (Narrator 1), October 17 (Narrator 4), and December 18 (Narrator 5 & 10) of the same year—less than four months from beginning to end.

overview.title: To whom was it written and why?
overview.content: Haggai directed his messages specifically to Zerubbabel, the governor of Judah, and to Jeshua the high priest (Hag 1:1). They were the top civil and religious leaders. In addressing them, Haggai was speaking to all the Jews whom they led—all those who had returned from exile. His purpose was simple and direct. He wanted them to see that they had robbed themselves of God’s blessings by allowing the temple building project to lie dormant. If they wanted to see God’s blessings flow again, they must finish restoring the temple.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: Eighteen years before Haggai’s prophecy, the Persian king Cyrus had allowed thousands of Jews to return from Babylon to Judah (538 bc, see Ezr 1:2-4). Although the Jews had begun rebuilding the temple sixteen years earlier, the opposition of neighboring peoples had intimidated them and caused them to abandon their work. The good news is that the people heeded Haggai’s exhortation and “began to work on the house of their God. . . on September 21” (Narrator 4)—just 23 days after Haggai’s initial prophecy called them to action. The task would be completed on March 12, three and a half years later (Ezr 6:15)—almost exactly seventy years after it had been destroyed by Nebuchadnezzer’s army.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
One item that clearly stands out with the SourceView colors is the blue text which records only two contributions from the priests. Both are only one word long. The first is “No.” The second is “Yes.” Their answers are given in response to a question prompted by God and posed by Haggai. The legal debate back and forth between them serves to clarify a point of religious law: whereas contact with a clean object doesn’t make you ceremonially clean, contact with an unclean object instantly makes you ceremonially unclean. The point is that it’s easier to be made unclean than it is to be made clean.

Right after that, Haggai challenges the people to “Think about this eighteenth day of December, the day when the foundation of the Lord’s temple was laid. Think carefully” (Haggai 2). Why? He wanted them to understand that it is harder to purify than to defile. To sin costs little. To redeem would cost much. That’s what the temple was about—the costly restoration of those who had become defiled.

Concerning that temple, God promised, “I will fill this place with glory” (God 4). But after the second temple was rebuilt and dedicated (Ezr 6:16-18) the glory of God did not fill it as it had when Solomon dedicated the first temple (2Ch 5:4-14). When, then, would God’s promise be fulfilled? When Jesus, the very glory of God, appeared in the temple. In fact it would be his own costly sacrifice that would ultimately fulfill all that Haggai and the people of his day had hoped for. Jesus is the greater glory that outshines the past glory of Solomon’s day. He would establish peace once and for all between God and all the peoples of all nations who would as a result bring their treasures before God to worship him for his costly redemption (Hag 2:7-9).

overview.title: How to read Zechariah.
overview.content:
Context makes all the difference. Read this book together with Ezra and Haggai—both of whom were contemporaries of Zechariah and partners with him in seeing the temple in Jerusalem rebuilt after the long exile in Babylon. The leaders and the people had become discouraged. Going home wasn’t turning out to be as wonderful as they thought it would be. There was a lot of work and a lot of opposition. Was it worth all the effort? Zechariah’s answer to them was an unqualified, “Yes!” His prophecies encouraged them to “be strong and finish the task!” (God 18).

The book begins with a call to repentance given by Zechariah in November, 520 bc. Then, between February15, 519 bc (1:7) and December 7, 518 bc (7:1), Zechariah had nine amazing—you might even say exotic—visions (1:8-6:15) that describe God’s powerful reign and his life-giving, redemptive acts. They were:
• Vision 1
• Vision 2
• Vision 3
• Vision 4
• Vision 5
• Vision 6
• Vision 7
• Vision 8
• Vision 9

If these visions seem a challenge to understand, don’t worry. Following them comes a series of messages in which God’s vision for Jerusalem and his redeemed people is crystal clear. He says: “My love for Mount Zion is passionate and strong; I am consumed with passion for Jerusalem! . . . I am returning to Mount Zion, and I will live in Jerusalem . . . Once again old men and women will walk Jerusalem’s streets with their canes . . . the city will be filled with boys and girls at play . . . I will bring them home again to live safely in Jerusalem . . . I am determined to bless Jerusalem and the people of Judah” (God 18). With this in mind, God reminds Zechariah’s hearers, “Among the other nations, Judah and Israel became symbols of a cursed nation. But no longer! Now I will rescue you and make you both a symbol and a source of blessing. So don’t be afraid. Be strong, and get on with rebuilding the Temple!” (God 18).

Tucked away amidst these closing messages are some amazing prophetic gems that predict precise details about the coming of Messiah, the ultimate restorer of God’s temple:

overview.title: Who wrote this book and when?
overview.content: Zechariah, whose name means “The Lord remembers,” was a prophet and a priest. He was born in exile. As a young man he returned from Babylon to Jerusalem. He began prophesying in 520 bc. See the introduction to Haggai, his prophetic companion, for more background information.

overview.title: What was happening at the time?
overview.content: God’s people had been in captivity for seventy years. When Cyrus of Persia came to power, he permitted the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the temple. Those who returned set about their task initially with great enthusiasm but soon encountered obstacles and became discouraged. Zechariah not only motivated them to finish what they had begun, but also gave them a vision of God’s purposes beyond the restored temple.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
One item that stands out when you read Zechariah is how much blue text there is—nearly three and a half times more than Jonah, who among the minor prophets is the book with the next most amount of blue. What makes this all the more interesting is that 96 percent of these blue words are spoken by an assortment of angelic beings. Now that’s an interesting supporting cast!

God’s words make up nearly half of the text. If you want to get to the meat of the message, one good way is to look at the imperatives. These often reflect the author’s desired take-aways. The list that follows does not include all the commands in the book, but only those (in red) spoken by God through Zechariah to the people of Jerusalem. As you read through them, ponder on present-day applications for your life:
• God 1
• God 5
• God 7
• God 11
• God 12
• God 17
• God 18

overview.title: How to read Malachi.
overview.content:
The crisis is past and the Israelites have slipped into passion-killing rituals. The awe of being restored to their homeland from exile has worn off, and they are simply going through the motions of religious activity. God’s questions expose the emptiness of their daily customs. As you listen to their dialogue, you may want to ask some questions inspired by the book: “Do I really believe he loves me? Does he have my wholehearted love and obedience? Or am I only going through the motions of faith?” Perhaps these questions can shake you too out of any monotonous routine and ignite a new passion for God.

The name Malachi means “messenger” in Hebrew. It is God’s message to God’s would-be messengers. The question-and-answer format used between God and his people highlights some key issues in the life of God’s messengers: their relationship with God, their family life, their use of money, their attitude toward ministry, the way they treat others, and their motivation for serving God. The questions provoke us to check our own commitment to a no-holds-barred faith, obedience, and worship.

Notice that in this verbal exchange, God is definitely not doing things out of a mechanical sense of duty. Observe how passionate God is in his communication. God loves us passionately. God cares for us passionately. That love—not religious duty—is to be the foundation of our intimacy with him. And he wants us to reciprocate that love by showing faithfulness in human relationships, by being people of integrity and purity, and by making sure that the motive for our religious service is the desire to honor God, not an effort to gain some personal benefit. Malachi does not simply evaluate external actions, but presses in to the inner workings of our heart. For this reason Malachi can be very disquieting, leaving you with no pretenses, no place to hide. Or it can be most transformational, as you allow God to use its words to expose the roots of ungodly attitudes and change the purposes of your heart.

overview.title: Who wrote this book?
overview.content:
Malachi is actually not a proper name. It is a common noun which means “my messenger” which may have been used as an honorific title. Prophets were often called “messengers” of the Lord (see Hag 1:13). Whether Malachi was his name or his title, this prophet clearly sensed that God was speaking through him.

Two passages in the book make a significant play on words with the Hebrew for messenger (malachi). They are:
• “The words of a priest’s lips should preserve knowledge of God, and people should go to him for instruction, for the priest is the messenger of the Lord of Heaven’s Armies” (Mal 2:7, emphasis added).
• “Look! I am sending my messenger, and he will prepare the way before me. Then the Lord you are seeking will suddenly come to his Temple. The messenger of the covenant, whom you look for so eagerly, is surely coming,” says the Lord of Heaven’s Armies (Mal 3:1, emphasis added).

overview.title: When was it written?
overview.content: This is the last book written before the New Testament era. It was composed sometime after 460 bc—after Israel returned from captivity in Babylon (538 bc), after the temple in Jerusalem had been rebuilt (516 bc), and after worship there had lapsed into mere routine.

overview.title: Why was it written?
overview.content: To confront the spirit of complacency and indifference that so easily overcomes God’s people and their spiritual leaders.

overview.title: SourceView Insights.
overview.content:
In Malachi you will find no green text and hardly any blue text. You will see that it has the second highest percentage of red text (God’s words) among the minor prophets (see the introduction to Hosea).

An outstanding highlight of this book is the intensity of the dialogue, as questions are raised and answers are given in a crisp back-and-forth interchange. In fact, The Israelites have four speaking parts—in which they ask five questions! Their words comprise more than 70 percent of the blue text and they never once say anything that is not a question!

Add to this the fact that The Narrator asks five questions and God asks seven questions, and you start to taste the flavor of this messenger’s message. But in addition to the seventeen questions already alluded to (can you find them all?) there are ten more. These are questions that the people have, but which God speaks out as he quotes them. Perhaps some of these questions had been whispered out loud as they chatted back and forth. Perhaps some of them were just the musings of their hearts. But all of them can lead you to a deeper reflection about the inner motivation of your own life. Consider these questions as you allow God to probe deep into your life story:
• God 4 But you ask, “How have we ever shown contempt for your name?”
• God 21 But you ask, “How can we return when we have never gone away? Should people cheat God?”
• God 25 But you say, “What do you mean? What have we said against you?”
You have said, “What’s the use of serving God? What have we gained by obeying his commands or by trying to show the Lord of Heaven’s Armies that we are sorry for our sins?”

overview.title: How to read Matthew.
overview.content:
The gospel of Matthew focuses on Jesus as the Messiah King who comes to establish his kingdom. The story begins by recounting Jesus’ family line and the circumstances around his birth. You might be surprised to discover that the opening genealogy contains within it a significant prophetic insight! Jesus’ genealogy has been edited by Matthew in such a way as to announce the beginning of God’s Jubilee (see Lev 25:8-55; 27:16-25).
